results.title_inferred: Mihoutao (Kiwifruit / Chinese Gooseberry) — Fruit Section Five
results.original_text:
【釋名】獼猴梨（《開寶》）、藤梨（同上）、時珍曰︰其形如梨，其色如桃，而獼猴喜食，故有諸名。閩人呼為陽桃。

【集解】志曰︰生山谷中。藤著樹生，葉圓有毛。其實形似雞卵大，其皮褐色，經霜始甘美可食。皮堪作紙。

宗奭曰︰今陝西永興軍南山甚多。枝條柔弱，高二、三丈，多附木而生。

其子十月爛熟，色淡綠，生則極酸。子繁細，其色如芥子。淺山傍道則有存者，深山則多為猴所食矣。

實

【氣味】酸、甘，寒，無毒。

藏器曰︰咸、酸，無毒。多食冷脾胃，動泄瀉。

宗奭曰︰有實熱者宜食之。太過，則令人臟寒作泄。
甘，滑，寒，無毒。

【主治】止暴渴，解煩熱，壓丹石，下石淋（《開寶》。詵曰︰並宜取瓤和蜜作煎食）。

調中下氣，主骨節風，癱緩不隨，長年白髮，野雞內痔病（藏器）。

藤中汁
熱壅反胃，和生薑汁服之。又下石淋（藏器）。

枝、葉
殺蟲。煮汁飼狗，療疥（《開寶》）。
results.translation:
**[Nomenclature]**
Alternate names: Míhóu lí [Macaque Pear] (from *Kaibao Bencao*); Téng lí [Vine Pear] (same source). Li Shizhen states: Its shape resembles a pear, its color resembles a peach, and macaques are fond of eating it — hence these various names. The people of Fujian call it Yángtáo.

**[Collected Explanations]**
Zhi [Su Song or Chen Zangqi, attributed author] states: It grows in mountain valleys. The vine climbs and clings to trees. Its leaves are round and hairy. The fruit is approximately the size of a chicken's egg, with brown skin; it becomes sweet and pleasant to eat only after the first frost. The skin can be made into paper.

Kou Zongshi states: It is found in great abundance today in the southern mountains of Yongxing Prefecture, Shaanxi. Its branches are slender and pliable, growing two to three zhang [roughly 6–10 meters] high, mostly climbing upon other trees. The fruit fully ripens in the tenth month, turning pale green; when unripe it is extremely sour. The seeds are numerous and tiny, their color resembling mustard seeds. The fruit can still be found on foothills along roadsides, but in deep mountains it is mostly consumed by monkeys.

---

**Fruit**

**[Flavor and Nature]**
Sour, sweet; cold; non-toxic.

Chen Zangqi states: Salty, sour; non-toxic. Eaten in excess, it chills the spleen and stomach and may provoke diarrhea.

Kou Zongshi states: Those with excess-heat conditions are well-suited to eat it. Eaten in excess, however, it causes cold in the viscera and induces diarrhea.
[Also noted as:] Sweet, slippery [smooth], cold; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Stops sudden intense thirst; relieves vexing heat; counteracts the toxicity of mineral [elixir] drugs [dān shí, i.e., cinnabar and other alchemical substances]; expels urinary stone [shí lín, strangury with calculi]. (*Kaibao Bencao*. Meng Shen states: It is best to extract the pulp, mix with honey, and prepare it as a decocted food.)
- Regulates the middle [burner] and descends qi; treats wind in the joints [gǔ jié fēng, arthritic or rheumatic conditions]; paralysis and limpness with loss of voluntary movement; long-standing white hair; pheasant-gut hemorrhoids [yě jī nèi zhì, a type of internal hemorrhoid]. (Chen Zangqi)

---

**Sap from the Vine**

[Condition treated:] Heat obstruction causing regurgitation [fǎn wèi, reflux/vomiting]. [Preparation:] Mix the vine sap with fresh ginger juice and take orally. Also expels urinary stone [shí lín]. (Chen Zangqi)

---

**Branches and Leaves**

Kill parasites/worms. Boil into a decoction and feed to dogs to treat mange and scabies. (*Kaibao Bencao*)

entities_mentioned.name: 獼猴桃 (Míhóu táo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Actinidia deliciosa or Actinidia chinensis (kiwifruit / Chinese gooseberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: All morphological and ecological descriptions — egg-sized brown-skinned fruit, hairy round leaves, vine habit, sour when unripe, ripening in late autumn — correspond well to wild Actinidia species native to central and southern China.

entities_mentioned.name: 陽桃 (Yángtáo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Local Fujianese name for Actinidia; distinct from the modern usage of 楊桃 (Averrhoa carambola, starfruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: In this context 陽桃 is a regional synonym for 獼猴桃, not the starfruit. The modern reassignment of 楊桃 to Averrhoa carambola is a later development.

entities_mentioned.name: 丹石 (dān shí)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alchemical mineral drugs (e.g., cinnabar, arsenolite, sulfur compounds)
entities_mentioned.notes: A common category in medieval Chinese pharmacology referring to toxic elixir minerals used in Daoist alchemy; the fruit was believed to counteract their harmful effects.

entities_mentioned.name: 石淋 (shí lín)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urolithiasis (urinary calculi / kidney stones)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical TCM diagnostic category corresponding to strangury caused by stone formation in the urinary tract.

entities_mentioned.name: 骨節風 (gǔ jié fēng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arthritic or rheumatic joint disease
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'wind of the bone joints'; a broad classical category encompassing inflammatory and degenerative joint conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 野雞內痔 (yě jī nèi zhì)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Internal hemorrhoids (a specific classical subtype)
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'pheasant-gut internal hemorrhoids'; a classical typological designation for a form of internal hemorrhoid.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text is truncated at the end (the branch/leaf section citation reference '《' is incomplete); the text has been completed as '(*Kaibao Bencao*)' based on context and parallel passages. 2. '動泄' in the original is expanded to '動泄瀉' (provoke diarrhea) as the character '瀉' appears to have been lost to OCR truncation. 3. The attribution of individual statements follows the standard editorial convention of the Bencao Gangmu, where named commentators (藏器 = Chen Zangqi, 宗奭 = Kou Zongshi, 詵 = Meng Shen, 志 = likely Su Song or the Kaibao editors) are cited inline. 4. '高二、三丈' is rendered as approximately 6–10 meters (one zhang ≈ 3.3 m in the Ming period).

【釋名】竿蔗（《草木狀》）、薯（音遮）。

時珍曰︰按︰《野史》云︰呂惠卿言︰凡草皆正生嫡出，惟蔗側種，根上庶出，故字從庶也。嵇含作竿蔗，謂其莖如竹竿也。《離騷》、《漢書》皆作柘字，通用也。薯字出許慎《說文》，蓋蔗音之轉也。

【集解】弘景曰︰蔗出江東為勝，廬陵亦有好者。廣州一種，數年生皆大如竹，長丈餘，取汁為沙糖，甚益人。又有荻蔗，節疏而細，亦可啖也。

頌曰︰今江浙、閩廣、湖南、蜀川所生，大者亦高丈許。有二種︰荻蔗其葉似荻，莖細短而節疏，但堪生啖，亦可煎稀糖；竹蔗莖粗而長，可榨汁為沙糖，泉、福、吉、廣諸州多作之。煉沙糖和牛乳為乳糖，惟蜀川作之。南人販至北地者，荻蔗多而竹蔗少也。

詵曰︰蔗有赤色者名昆侖蔗；白色者名荻蔗。竹蔗以蜀及嶺南者為勝，江東雖有而劣於蜀產。會稽所作乳糖，殆勝於蜀。

  "original_text": "【釋名】竿蔗（《草木狀》）、薯（音遮）。

時珍曰︰按︰《野史》云︰呂惠卿言︰凡草皆正生嫡出，惟蔗側種，根上庶出，故字從庶也。嵇含作竿蔗，謂其莖如竹竿也。《離騷》、《漢書》皆作柘字，通用也。薯字出許慎《說文》，蓋蔗音之轉也。

【集解】弘景曰︰蔗出江東為勝，廬陵亦有好者。廣州一種，數年生皆大如竹，長丈餘，取汁為沙糖，甚益人。又有荻蔗，節疏而細，亦可啖也。

頌曰︰今江浙、閩廣、湖南、蜀川所生，大者亦高丈許。有二種︰荻蔗其葉似荻，莖細短而節疏，但堪生啖，亦可煎稀糖；竹蔗莖粗而長，可榨汁為沙糖，泉、福、吉、廣諸州多作之。煉沙糖和牛乳為乳糖，惟蜀川作之。南人販至北地者，荻蔗多而竹蔗少也。

詵曰︰蔗有赤色者名昆侖蔗；白色者名荻蔗。竹蔗以蜀及嶺南者為勝，江東雖有而劣於蜀產。會稽所作乳糖，殆勝於蜀。

時珍曰︰蔗皆畦種，叢生，最困地力。莖似竹而內實，大者圍數寸，長六、七尺，根下節密，以漸而疏。抽葉如蘆葉而大，長三、四尺，扶疏四垂。八、九月收莖，可留過春充果食。按王灼《糖霜譜》云︰蔗有四色︰曰杜蔗，即竹蔗也，綠嫩薄皮，味極醇濃，專用作霜；曰西蔗，作霜色淺；曰蔗，亦名蠟蔗，即荻蔗也，亦可作沙糖；曰紅蔗，亦名紫蔗，即昆侖蔗也，止可生啖，不堪作糖。

蔗

【氣味】甘，平，澀，無毒。

大明曰︰冷。

詵曰︰共酒食，發痰。

瑞曰︰多食，發虛熱，動衄血。

【主治】下氣和中，助脾氣，利大腸（《別錄》）。利大小腸，消痰止渴，除心胸煩熱，解酒毒。燒存性，研末，烏麻油調，塗小兒頭瘡白禿，頻塗取瘥。燒煙勿令入人目，能使暗明（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰蔗，脾之果也。其漿甘寒，能瀉火熱，《素問》所謂甘溫除大熱之意。煎煉成糖，則甘溫而助濕熱，所謂積溫成熱也。蔗漿消渴解酒，自古稱之。故《漢書‧郊祀歌》云︰百末旨酒布蘭生，泰尊柘漿析朝酲。唐王維《櫻桃詩》云︰飽食不須愁內熱，大官還有蔗漿寒。是矣。而孟詵乃謂共酒食發痰者，豈不知其有解酒除熱之功耶？日華子大明又謂沙糖能解酒毒，則不知既經煎煉，便能助酒為熱，與生漿之性異矣。按︰晁氏《客話》云︰甘草遇火則熱，麻油遇火則冷，甘蔗煎飴則熱，水成湯則冷。此物性之異，醫者可不知乎？又《野史》云︰盧絳中病疾疲瘵，忽夢白衣婦人云︰食蔗可愈。及旦買蔗數挺食之，翌日疾愈。此亦助脾和中之驗歟？",
  "translation": "**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Also called *ganzhe* [bamboo-pole cane] (in *Caomuzhuang* [Record of Plants and Trees]) and *zhe* (pronounced *zhe* [遮]).

Li Shizhen states: According to the *Yeshi* [Unofficial History], Lü Huiqing said: All grasses grow upright from a central shoot, but sugarcane is planted sideways and sprouts laterally from the root — hence the character [蔗] incorporates the element 庶 [meaning 'lateral/subordinate offspring']. Ji Han [author of *Caomuzhuang*] wrote it as *ganzhe* [竿蔗], likening its stalk to a bamboo pole. In the *Li Sao* and the *Han Shu* [History of the Han], the character 柘 is used interchangeably. The form 薯 appears in Xu Shen's *Shuowen Jiezi* [Explaining Simple and Compound Characters] and is a phonetic variant of *zhe*.

**[Collected Explanations]**

results.original_text:
【集解】恭曰：沙糖，蜀地、西戎、江東並有之。榨甘蔗汁煎成，紫色。瑞曰：稀者為蔗糖，乾者為沙糖，球者為球糖，餅者為糖餅。沙糖中凝結如石，破之如沙，透明白者，為糖霜。

時珍曰：此紫沙糖也。法出西域，唐太宗始遣人傳其法入中國。以蔗汁過樟木槽，取而煎成。清者為蔗糖，凝結有沙者為沙糖。漆瓮造成，如石、如霜、如冰者，為石蜜、為糖霜、為冰糖也。紫糖亦可煎化，印成鳥獸果物之狀，以充席獻。今之貨者，又多雜以米餳諸物，不可不知。

【氣味】甘，寒，無毒。

恭曰：冷利過於石蜜。

詵曰：性溫不冷。多食令人心痛，生長蟲，消肌肉，損齒，發疳。與鯽魚同食，成疳蟲；與葵同食，生流；與筍同食，

【主治】心腹熱脹，口乾渴（《唐本》）。潤心肺大小腸熱，解酒毒。臘月瓶封窖糞坑中，患天行熱狂者，絞汁服，甚良（大明）。和中助脾。

【發明】宗奭曰：蔗汁清，故費煎煉致紫黑色。今醫家治暴熱，多用為先導；兼啖駝、馬，解熱。小兒多食則損齒生蟲者，土製水，蟲屬土，得甘即生也。

震亨曰：糖生胃火，乃濕土生熱，故能損齒生蟲，與食棗病齲同意，非土製水也。

時珍曰：沙糖性溫，殊於蔗漿，故不宜多食。與魚、筍之類同食，皆不益人。今人每用為調和，徒取其適口，而不知陰受其害也。但其性能和脾緩肝，故治脾胃及瀉肝藥用為先導。本草言其性寒，蘇恭謂其冷利，皆昧此理。
results.translation:
**Comprehensive Commentaries**

Su Gong states: Sand sugar [shātáng] is produced in Shu [Sichuan], among the Western Rong peoples, and in Jiangdong. It is made by pressing sugarcane juice and boiling it down; the result is purple in color.

Wu Rui states: The liquid form is called cane sugar [zhètáng]; the dry form is sand sugar [shātáng]; the spherical form is ball sugar [qiútáng]; the flat form is sugar cake [tángbǐng]. When sand sugar congeals within itself into a stone-like mass that, when broken, crumbles like sand and is translucent and white, it is called sugar frost [tángshuāng].

Li Shizhen states: This is purple sand sugar. The method of making it originated in the Western Regions [Central Asia], and it was only during the reign of Emperor Taizong of the Tang dynasty that envoys were sent to bring the technique to China. Cane juice is passed through troughs made of camphor wood and then boiled down. The clear product is cane sugar; the product that congeals with a sandy texture is sand sugar. When made in lacquered vats and the result resembles stone, frost, or ice, it is called stone honey [shímì], sugar frost [tángshuāng], or rock candy [bīngtáng], respectively. Purple sugar can also be melted down and molded into the shapes of birds, beasts, fruits, and plants for use as offerings at banquets. Today's commercial sugar is often adulterated with grain syrup [mǐ táng] and other substances — one must be aware of this.

**Flavor and Nature**

Sweet; cold; non-toxic.

Su Gong states: Its cooling and purgative effect surpasses that of stone honey [shímì].

Meng Shen states: Its nature is warm, not cold. Eating too much causes heartache [cardiac/epigastric pain], generates intestinal worms, wastes the muscles, damages the teeth, and gives rise to gan [childhood nutritional disorders / parasitic ailments]. Eaten together with crucian carp, it produces gan worms; eaten together with mallow [kuí], it causes running sores [liú]; eaten together with bamboo shoots... [text incomplete]

**Indications and Treatments**

Heat and distension in the heart and abdomen; dry mouth and thirst (Tang Bencao). Moistens heat in the heart, lungs, large and small intestines; resolves alcohol toxicity. In the twelfth lunar month, sealed in a bottle and buried in a cesspit — for patients suffering from epidemic febrile mania [tiānxíng rè kuáng], wring out the juice and administer orally; this is highly effective (Rihuazi Bencao). Harmonizes the middle [jiao] and supports the spleen.

**Elucidations**

Kou Zongshi states: Cane juice is clear, so much boiling and refining is required to produce the purple-black color [of sand sugar]. Physicians today frequently use it as a leading agent in treating acute heat [conditions]; it is also fed to camels and horses to relieve heat. The reason excessive consumption by children damages teeth and generates worms is that earth [in Five Phases theory] restrains water, and worms belong to earth — they are generated whenever sweetness [associated with earth] is present.

Zhu Zhenheng states: Sugar generates stomach fire because damp earth generates heat. This is why it damages teeth and generates worms — the same reasoning as why eating dates causes tooth decay. It is not a case of earth restraining water.

Li Shizhen states: The nature of sand sugar is warm, quite different from fresh cane juice, and therefore it should not be consumed in excess. Eating it together with fish, bamboo shoots, and the like is all harmful. People today habitually use it as a seasoning, caring only for its pleasing taste, unaware that they are suffering harm in a gradual, hidden way. However, its nature can harmonize the spleen and relax the liver; therefore it is used as a leading agent in medicines that treat the spleen and stomach or that drain the liver. The Bencao texts that say its nature is cold, and Su Gong's claim that it is cooling and purgative, both fail to grasp this principle.

entities_mentioned.name: 沙糖 (shātáng, sand sugar / brown cane sugar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Raw or partially refined cane sugar (Saccharum officinarum L.), roughly equivalent to muscovado or raw brown sugar
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished in the text from cane juice (蔗糖), rock candy (冰糖), stone honey (石蜜), and sugar frost (糖霜). The 'purple' color indicates partially refined brown sugar retaining molasses.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘蔗 (sugarcane)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saccharum officinarum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: The raw material from which sand sugar is pressed and boiled.

entities_mentioned.name: 糖霜 (tángshuāng, sugar frost)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crystallized white sugar or rock sugar in early form
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the transparent, white crystalline product formed within sand sugar; analogous to an early form of refined crystalline sugar.

entities_mentioned.name: 石蜜 (shímì, stone honey)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hard refined sugar / sugar candy
entities_mentioned.notes: A harder, stone-like form of solidified sugar made in lacquered vats; distinct from bee honey.

entities_mentioned.name: 冰糖 (bīngtáng, rock candy / ice sugar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rock candy (large crystalline refined sugar)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the product resembling ice, made from boiled cane juice congealed in lacquered vats.

entities_mentioned.name: 鯽魚 (crucian carp)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carassius auratus / Carassius carassius
entities_mentioned.notes: Warned against being eaten together with sand sugar, as the combination is said to produce gan worms.

entities_mentioned.name: 葵 (kuí, mallow)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malva verticillata L. or related Malva spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Warned against being eaten together with sand sugar, said to cause 'running sores' (流).

entities_mentioned.name: 筍 (bamboo shoots)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Shoots of various Bambusoideae spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Warned against being eaten together with sand sugar; text is incomplete at this point.

entities_mentioned.name: 疳 (gān, childhood nutritional disorders)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Broadly: parasitic and nutritional deficiency disorders in children (including infantile malnutrition, intestinal parasitosis)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Chinese disease category encompassing wasting, parasitic infection, and related conditions in children.

entities_mentioned.name: 天行熱狂 (epidemic febrile mania)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Febrile delirium or encephalitis associated with epidemic disease
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical syndrome of high fever with mental derangement caused by epidemic pathogens.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text contains significant repetition: the 【主治】and 【發明】sections appear duplicated, likely a scribal/OCR artifact. The cleaned original text retains only one set of each section. 2. In the 【氣味】section, Meng Shen's passage about eating sand sugar together with mallow (葵) causing 'liú' (流) and together with bamboo shoots is abruptly cut off — text appears incomplete in the source; the bamboo-shoots warning consequence is missing. 3. The Five Phases debate between Kou Zongshi (earth restrains water) and Zhu Zhenheng (damp earth generates heat) regarding why sugar damages teeth reflects a standard neo-Confucian medical discourse on elemental theory. 4. The method of burying sugar sealed in a bottle in a cesspit during the twelfth month to treat epidemic febrile mania likely refers to a fermented or chemically altered preparation — the mechanism is obscure by modern standards. 5. 'Gan worms' (疳蟲) is a classical term for intestinal parasites associated with the gan syndrome. 6. Li Shizhen's note that the technique was introduced to China by envoys sent by Emperor Taizong of Tang corresponds to accounts in the Old and New Tang Histories regarding diplomatic exchange with Magadha (India) ca. 648 CE.

【釋名】白沙糖。

恭曰︰石蜜即乳糖也，與蟲部石蜜同名。

時珍曰︰按︰萬震《涼州異物志》云︰石蜜非石類，假石之名也。實乃甘蔗汁煎而曝之，則凝如石而體甚輕，故謂之石蜜也。

【集解】志約曰︰石蜜出益州及西戎，煎煉沙糖為之，可作餅塊，黃白色。

恭曰︰石蜜用水、牛乳、米粉和煎成塊，作餅堅重。西戎來者佳，江左亦有，殆勝於蜀。

詵曰︰自蜀中、波斯來者良。東吳亦有，不及兩處者。皆煎蔗汁、牛乳，則易細白耳。

宗奭曰︰石蜜，川、浙者最佳，其味濃，他處皆次之，煎煉以型象物，達京師。至夏月及久陰雨，多自消化。土人先以竹葉及紙裹包，外用石夾埋之，不得見風，遂可免。今人謂之乳糖。其作餅黃白色者，謂之捻糖，易消化，入藥至少。

時珍曰︰石蜜，即白沙糖也。凝結作餅塊如石者為石蜜，輕白如霜者為糖霜，堅白如冰者為冰糖，皆一物有精粗之異也。以白糖煎化，模印成人物獅象之形者為饗糖，《後漢書注》所謂猊糖是也。以石蜜和諸果仁，及橙橘皮、縮砂、薄荷之類，作成餅塊者，為糖纏。以石蜜和牛乳、酥酪作成餅塊者，為乳糖。皆一物數變也。《唐本草》明言石蜜煎沙糖為之，而諸注皆以乳糖即為石蜜，殊欠分明。按︰王灼《糖霜譜》云︰古者惟飲蔗

【釋名】草蜜（《拾遺》）、給勃羅。

【集解】藏器曰︰交河沙中有草，頭上有毛，毛中生蜜。胡人名為給勃羅。

時珍曰︰按李延壽《北史》云︰高昌有草名羊刺，其上生蜜，味甚甘美。又《梁四公子記》云︰高昌貢刺蜜。傑公云︰南平城羊刺無葉，其蜜色白而味甘；鹽城羊刺葉大，其蜜色青而味薄也。高昌即交河，在西番，今為火州。又段成式《酉陽雜俎》云︰北天竺國有蜜草，蔓生大葉，秋冬不死，因受霜露，遂成蜜也。又《大明一統志》云︰西番撒馬兒罕地，有小草叢生，葉細如藍，秋露凝其上，味甘如蜜，可熬為餳，土人呼為達即古賓，蓋甘露也。按此二說，皆草蜜也，但不知其草即羊刺否也？又有KT 齊樹，亦出蜜，云可入藥而不得其詳，今附於下。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】骨蒸發熱痰嗽，暴痢下血，開胃止渴除煩（藏器）。

Category:果樹

，其實蓮（同上），其莖葉荷。

韓保升曰︰藕生水中，其葉名荷。按︰《爾雅》云︰荷，芙蕖。其莖茄，其葉 ，其本，其華菡萏，其實蓮，其根藕，其中 ， 中薏。邢 注云︰芙蕖，總名也，別名芙蓉，江東人呼為荷。菡萏，蓮花也。 ，蓮實也。薏， 中青心也。郭璞注云︰ ，乃莖下白在泥中者。蓮，乃房也； ，乃子也；薏，乃中心苦薏也。江東人呼荷花為芙蓉，北人以藕為荷，亦以蓮為荷，蜀人以藕為茄，此皆習俗傳誤也。陸機《詩疏》云︰其莖為荷。其花未發為菡萏，已發為芙蕖。其實蓮，蓮之皮青裡白。其子分，為苦薏也。

時珍曰︰《爾雅》以荷為根名，韓氏以荷為葉名，陸機以荷為莖名。按︰莖乃負葉者也，有負荷之義，當從陸說。 乃嫩 ，如竹之行鞭者。節生二莖，一為葉，一為花，盡處乃生藕，為花、葉、根、實之本。顯仁藏用，功成不居，可謂退藏於密矣，故謂之 。花葉常偶生，不偶不生，故根曰藕。或云藕善耕泥，故字從耦，耦者耕也。茄音加，加於 上也。

音遐，遠於密也。菡萏，函合未發之意。芙蓉，敷布容艷之意。蓮者連也，花實相連而出也。

時珍曰：《爾雅》以荷為根名，韓氏以荷為葉名，陸機以荷為莖名。按：莖乃負葉者也，有負荷之義，當從陸說。乃嫩，如竹之行鞭者。節生二莖，一為葉，一為花，盡處乃生藕，為花、葉、根、實之本。顯仁藏用，功成不居，可謂退藏於密矣，故謂之。花葉常偶生，不偶不生，故根曰藕。或云藕善耕泥，故字從耦，耦者耕也。茄音加，加於上也。音遐，遠於密也。菡萏，函合未發之意。芙蓉，敷布容艷之意。蓮者連也，花實相連而出也。者的也，子在房中點點如的也。的乃凡物點注之名。薏，猶意也，含苦在內也。古詩云：食子

【集解】《別錄》曰：藕實莖生汝南池澤。八月採。

當之曰：所在池澤皆有，豫章、汝南者良。苗高五、六尺，葉團青大如扇，其花赤，子黑如羊矢。

時珍曰：蓮藕，荊、揚、豫、益諸處湖澤陂池皆有之。以蓮子種者生遲，藕芽種者最易發。其芽穿泥成白，即也。長者至丈餘，五、六月嫩時，沒水取之，可作蔬茹，俗呼藕絲菜。節生二莖：一為藕荷，其葉貼水，其下旁行生藕也；一為芰荷，其葉出水，其旁莖生花也。其葉清明後生。六、七月開花，花有紅、白、粉紅三色。花心有黃須，蕊長寸余，須內即蓮也。花褪連房成，在房如蜂子在窠之狀。六、七月採嫩者，生食脆美。至秋房枯子黑，其堅如石，謂之石蓮子。八、九月收之，斫去黑殼，貨之四方，謂之蓮肉。冬月至春掘藕食之，藕白有孔有絲，大者如肱臂，長六、七尺，凡五、六節。大抵野生及紅花者，蓮多藕劣；種植及白花者，蓮少藕佳也。其花白者香，紅者艷，千葉者不結實。別有合歡（並頭者），有夜舒荷（夜布晝卷）、睡蓮（花夜入水）、金蓮（花黃）、碧蓮（花碧）、繡蓮（花如繡），皆是異種，故不述。《相感志》云：荷梗塞穴鼠自

蓮實

【氣味】甘，時珍曰：嫩性平，石蓮性溫。得茯苓、山藥、白朮、枸杞子良。

詵曰：生食過多，微動冷氣脹人。蒸食甚良。大便燥澀者，不可食。
甘，平，無毒。大明曰：溫。

時珍曰：《相感志》云：藕以鹽水共食，則不損口；同油炸面米果食，則無渣。煮忌鐵器。
甘，平，無毒。
澀，平，無毒。

大明曰：冷。伏硫黃。
苦，寒，無毒。

藏器曰：食蓮子不去心，令人作吐。
甘，澀，溫，無毒。

大明曰：忌地黃、蔥、蒜。
苦、甘，溫，無毒。忌地黃、蔥、蒜。
苦，澀，溫，無毒。
苦，平，無毒。

時珍曰：畏桐油。伏白銀，伏硫黃。

【主治】補中養神，益氣力，除百疾。久服，輕身耐老，不飢延年（不足，傷中氣絕，益十二經脈血氣（孟詵）。

止渴去熱，安心止痢，治腰痛及泄精。多食令人歡喜（大明）。交心腎，濃腸胃，固精氣，強筋骨，補虛損，利耳目，除寒濕，止脾泄久痢，赤白濁，女人帶下崩中諸血病（時珍）。

搗碎和米作粥飯食，輕身益氣，令人強健
熱渴，散留血，生肌。久服令人心歡（《別錄》）。止怒止泄，消食解酒毒，及病後乾渴（藏器）。搗汁服，止悶除煩開胃，治霍亂，破產後血悶，搗膏，金瘡並傷折，止暴痛。蒸煮食之，大能開胃（大明）。生食，治霍亂後虛渴。蒸食，甚補五臟，實下焦。

同蜜食，令人腹臟肥，不生諸蟲，亦可休糧（孟詵）。汁：解射罔毒、蟹毒（徐之才）。搗浸澄粉服食
生食，主霍亂後虛渴煩悶不能食，解酒食毒（蘇頌）。

功與藕同（時珍）。解煩毒，下瘀血（汪穎）。

藕節
搗汁飲，主吐血不止，及口鼻出血（甄權）。消瘀血，解熱毒。產後血悶，和地黃研汁
血渴，產後渴，生研末，米飲服二錢，立愈（士良）。止霍亂（大明）。清心去熱（時珍。出《統旨》）。

results.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰︰紅蓮花、白蓮花，生西國，胡人將來也。

時珍曰︰此不知即蓮花否？而功與蓮同，以類相從，姑移入此。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】久服，令人好顏色，變白卻老（藏器）。
results.translation:
【Collected Commentaries】Chen Cangqi states: The red lotus flower and white lotus flower grow in the Western Lands [西國, i.e., Central or West Asia] and were brought here by foreign [Hu] peoples.

Li Shizhen states: It is uncertain whether these are the same as the [common Chinese] lotus flower. However, their properties and functions are the same as those of the lotus, and so, following the principle of grouping similar things together, I have provisionally placed them in this entry.

【Qi and Flavor】Sweet; balanced [neutral in temperature]; non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】Long-term consumption brightens the complexion, whitens [the skin], and retards aging. (Chen Cangqi)

entities_mentioned.name: 紅蓮花 (Red Lotus Flower)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nelumbo nucifera (red-flowered variety), or possibly an exotic Central/West Asian species of lotus or similar flower
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen himself was uncertain whether this referred to the common lotus (Nelumbo nucifera) or a distinct foreign species brought along the Silk Road routes. The 'Western Lands' origin suggests it may refer to an imported variety or a related plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 白蓮花 (White Lotus Flower)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nelumbo nucifera (white-flowered variety), or possibly Nymphaea lotus or another white aquatic flower of foreign origin
entities_mentioned.notes: Same uncertainty as the red lotus flower. Li Shizhen grouped it here due to functional similarity with the standard lotus.

results.translator_notes: The attribution '藏器曰' refers to Chen Cangqi (陳藏器), the Tang-dynasty pharmacologist and author of the Bencao Shiyi (本草拾遺). '西國' literally means 'Western Lands' and typically denotes regions to the west of China proper, ranging from Central Asia to India or beyond. '胡人將來也' indicates these plants were introduced by non-Han foreign peoples, consistent with Silk Road-era introductions. The medical claim of long-term use brightening the complexion and retarding aging is a conventional cosmetic/longevity attribution in classical Chinese materia medica. No specific preparation method is described in this segment.

【釋名】菱（《別錄》）、水栗（風俗通）

時珍曰︰其葉支散，故字從支。其角棱峭，故謂之菱，而俗呼為菱角也。昔人多不分別，惟伍安貧《武陵記》以三角、四角者為芰，兩角者為菱。《左傳》屈到嗜芰，即此物也。《爾雅》謂之厥 （音眉）。又許慎《說文》云︰菱，楚謂之芰，秦謂之 。楊氏《丹鉛錄》以芰為雞頭，引《離騷》緝芰荷以為衣，言菱葉不可緝衣，皆誤矣。案︰《爾雅》 乃決明之名，非厥 也。又《埤雅》芰荷乃藕上出水生花之莖，非雞頭也。與菱同名異物。許、楊二氏失於詳考

【集解】弘景曰︰芰實，廬、江間最多，皆取火燔以為米充糧，頌曰︰菱，處處有之。葉浮水上，花黃白色，花落而實生，漸向水中乃熟。實有二種︰一種四角，一種兩角。兩角中又有嫩皮而紫色者，謂之浮菱，食之尤美。江淮及山東人曝其實以為米，代糧。

時珍曰︰芰菱有湖濼處則有之。菱落泥中，最易生發。有野菱、家菱，皆三月生蔓延引。

葉浮水上，扁而有尖，光面如鏡。葉下之莖有股如蝦股，一莖一葉，兩兩相差，如蝶翅狀。

五、六月開小白花，背日而生，晝合宵炕，隨月轉移。其實有數種︰或三角、四角，或兩角、無角。野菱自生湖中，葉、實俱小。其角硬直刺人，其色嫩青老黑。嫩時

入染鬚髮方（時珍）。

烏菱殼

【釋名】鳧茈（音疵）、鳧茨（音瓷）、荸薺（《衍義》）、黑三棱（《博濟方》）、芍（音曉）。地栗（鄭樵《通志》）。

時珍曰︰烏芋，其根如芋而色烏也。鳧喜食之，故《爾雅》名鳧茈，後遂訛為鳧茨，又訛為荸薺。蓋切韻鳧、荸同一字母，音相近也。三棱、地栗，皆形似也。

瑞曰︰小者名鳧茈，大者名地栗。

【集解】頌曰︰烏芋，今鳧茨也。苗似龍須而細，色正青。根如指頭大，黑色，皮濃有毛。又有一種皮薄無毛者，亦同。田中人並食之。

宗奭曰︰皮濃色黑，肉硬而白者，謂之豬荸薺。皮薄澤，色淡紫，肉軟而脆者，謂之羊荸薺。正二月，人采食之。此二等藥

時珍曰︰鳧茈生淺水田中。其苗三、四月出土，一莖直上，無枝葉，狀如龍須。肥田栽者，粗近蔥、蒲，高二、三尺。其根白，秋後結顆，大如山楂、栗子，而臍有聚毛，累累下生入泥底。野生者，黑而小，食之多滓。種出者，紫而大，食之多毛。吳人以沃田種之，三月下種，霜後苗枯，冬春掘收為果，生食、煮食皆良。

【氣味】甘，微寒，滑，無毒。

詵曰︰性冷。先有冷氣人不可食，令人腹脹氣滿。小兒秋月食多，臍下結痛也。

【主治】消渴痺熱，溫中益氣（《別錄》）。下丹石，消風毒，除胸中實熱氣。可作粉食，明耳目，消黃膽（孟詵）。開胃下食（大明）。作粉食，濃人腸胃，不飢，能解毒，服金石人宜之（蘇頌）。療五種膈氣，消宿食，飯後宜食之。治誤吞銅物（汪機）。主血痢下血血崩，辟蠱毒（時珍）。

【發明】機曰︰烏芋善毀銅，合銅錢嚼之，則錢化，可見其為消堅削積之物。故能化五種膈疾，而消宿食，治誤吞銅也。時珍曰︰按︰王氏《博濟方》︰治五積、冷氣攻心、變為五膈諸病，金鎖丸中用黑三棱。注云︰即鳧茈乾者。則汪氏所謂消堅之說，蓋本於此。又董炳《集驗方》云︰地栗晒乾為末，白湯每服二錢，能辟蠱毒。傳聞下蠱之家，知有此物，便不敢下。此亦前人所未知者。
results.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**

Alternate names: Fúcí [鳧茈, pronounced 'cí'], Fúcí [鳧茨, pronounced 'cí'], Bíqí [荸薺] (from the *Bencao Yanyi*), Hēi Sānléng [黑三棱, 'Black Water Plantain'] (from the *Boji Fang*), and Sháo [芍, pronounced 'xiǎo']. Also known as Dìlì [地栗, 'Earth Chestnut'] (from Zheng Qiao's *Tongzhi*).

Li Shizhen states: The name *wūyù* [烏芋] comes from its root resembling a taro [yù] but being dark [wū] in color. Wild ducks [fú] are fond of eating it, hence the *Erya* [classical lexicon] calls it *fúcí*; this was later corrupted into *fúcí* [鳧茨], and further corrupted into *bíqí* [荸薺]. This is because, in rhyming tables [qieyun], the characters *fú* [鳧] and *bí* [荸] share the same initial consonant class and have similar sounds. The names *sānléng* [three-edged] and *dìlì* [earth chestnut] both derive from its shape.

Wu Rui states: The small variety is called *fúcí*, and the large variety is called *dìlì*.

**Collected Explanations**

Su Song states: *Wūyù* is what is today called *fúcí*. Its shoots resemble horsetail rush [lóngsū] but are finer, and are a true green in color. The root is about the size of a fingertip, black in color, with a thick skin covered in hair. There is also a variety with a thin, smooth skin and no hair, which is otherwise the same. The people of the fields commonly eat both.

Kou Zongshi states: The variety with a thick, black skin and firm white flesh is called *zhū bíqí* ['pig water chestnut']. The variety with a thin, lustrous skin, light purple in color, and soft, crisp flesh is called *yáng bíqí* ['sheep water chestnut']. People gather and eat them in the first and second months [of the lunar year]. These two varieties are [used as] medicine ...

Li Shizhen states: *Fúcí* grows in shallow water paddies. Its shoots emerge from the soil in the third or fourth month, growing as a single straight stem with no branches or leaves, resembling horsetail rush in appearance. When cultivated in rich soil, the stalks are as thick as scallions or bulrush, reaching two to three feet in height. The root is white; after autumn it forms tubers as large as hawthorn berries or chestnuts, with a cluster of hair at the navel, growing in chains downward into the mud at the bottom. Wild plants are black and small, and produce many dregs when eaten. Cultivated plants are purple and large, and have more fiber when eaten. The people of Wu [the Suzhou/Jiangnan region] cultivate them in fertile fields, planting in the third month. After the first frost the shoots wither; in winter and spring the tubers are dug up and used as fruit, and are equally good eaten raw or cooked.

**Flavor and Properties**

Sweet, slightly cold, slippery [in texture], non-toxic.

Meng Shen states: Its nature is cold. People who already suffer from cold-qi conditions should not eat it, as it will cause abdominal bloating and fullness. If children eat too much of it in the autumn months, it will cause cramping and pain below the navel.

**Indications and Treatments**

- Eliminates wasting-thirst [xiāokě], bi-heat [pìrè], and warms the center [middle jiao] and replenishes qi (from the *Bielu*).
- Expels [the toxic effects of] elixir minerals [dānshí], disperses wind-toxin [fēngdú], removes excess heat-qi from the chest. Can be made into a flour for eating; brightens the ears and eyes; resolves jaundice [huángdǎn] (Meng Shen).
- Opens the stomach and promotes the descent of food (from the *Da Ming* [Rihuazi Bencao]).
- Made into a flour for eating, it thickens the intestines and stomach, prevents hunger, and can neutralize toxins; it is especially suitable for those taking mineral preparations [jīnshí] (Su Song).
- Treats the five types of diaphragm-qi [géqì], disperses food stagnation; it is suitable to eat after meals. Treats the accidental swallowing of copper objects (Wang Ji).
- Governs blood dysentery, bloody stools, and uterine hemorrhage [xuèbēng]; repels gǔ-poison [蠱毒] (Li Shizhen).

**Elaborations**

Wang Ji states: *Wūyù* has a remarkable ability to destroy copper: if you chew it together with a copper coin, the coin will dissolve. From this one can see that it is a substance that breaks down hard accumulations. Hence it can resolve the five types of diaphragm ailments, disperse food stagnation, and treat the accidental swallowing of copper.

Li Shizhen states: According to Wang's *Boji Fang*: for treating the five accumulations, cold-qi attacking the heart, and its transformation into the five diaphragm diseases, the formula Jinsuo Wan [Golden Lock Pill] uses Hēi Sānléng [Black Three-Edge]. A note in the text says this is simply dried *fúcí*. Thus the theory of 'breaking down hardness' put forward by Wang Ji is evidently based on this source. Furthermore, Dong Bing's *Jiyan Fang* [Collected Proven Formulas] states: Earth chestnuts [dìlì], sun-dried and ground into powder, taken in doses of two qian [approx. 6g] with plain hot water [báitāng], can repel gǔ-poison. It is said that those who traffic in gǔ-poison, upon learning that a household possesses this substance, dare not deploy their poison. This too is something that was unknown to earlier writers.

entities_mentioned.name: 烏芋 / 鳧茈 / 荸薺 / 地栗
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Water chestnut (Eleocharis dulcis) or possibly the closely related E. tuberosa; the description matches the Chinese water chestnut cultivated throughout East and Southeast Asia
entities_mentioned.notes: The text distinguishes two cultivated varieties ('pig' and 'sheep' water chestnut) and wild versus cultivated plants. The plant described — a single leafless rush-like stem growing in shallow water, producing rounded dark corms — is clearly Eleocharis dulcis. The name 黑三棱 (Black Three-Edge) should not be confused with Sparganium (true sānléng / 三棱), which is a different plant also used in Chinese medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 黑三棱
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Used here as a synonym for dried water chestnut (Eleocharis dulcis), not Sparganium stoloniferum
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen explicitly cites the Boji Fang annotation clarifying that 黑三棱 in the Jinsuo Wan formula refers to dried fúcí (water chestnut), not the true aquatic sānléng herb.

results.translator_notes: 1. The text for the Kou Zongshi passage ends abruptly ('此二等藥'), suggesting the original or OCR source is missing continuation text; this has been translated as-is with a bracketed ellipsis. 2. 'Gǔ-poison' (蠱毒) refers to a feared category of magically-administered poisoning in traditional Chinese culture; it is not a biomedical concept and has been left in transliteration with a gloss. 3. The claim that water chestnuts can dissolve copper coins when chewed together reflects a traditional Chinese pharmacological concept of 'dispersing hard accumulations' (消堅) rather than a literal chemical observation. 4. 'Five diaphragm diseases' (五膈) refers to a classical TCM categorization of upper digestive obstruction syndromes. 5. The character 白 followed by a space in the original ('其根白 ，秋後結顆') appears to be an OCR artifact; context suggests the full phrase may have been '其根白嫩' (roots white and tender) or similar, but since the intended word is not unambiguous, it has been rendered simply as 'white'.

results.title_inferred: Arrowhead (Cígu / 慈菇) — Root and Leaf
results.original_text:
【釋名】借姑（《別錄》）、水萍（《別錄》）、河鳧茈（《圖經》）、白地栗（同上），苗名剪刀草。根歲生十二子，如慈菇之乳諸子，故以名之。作茨菰者非矣。河鳧茈、白地栗，所以別烏芋之鳧茈、地栗也。剪刀、箭搭、槎丫、燕尾。

【集解】《別錄》曰：借姑，三月三日采根，曝乾。

弘景曰：借姑生水田中。葉有丫，狀如澤瀉。其根黃，似芋子而小，煮之可啖。

恭曰：慈菇生水中。葉似箭之鏃，澤瀉之類也。

頌曰：剪刀草，生江湖及汴洛近水河溝沙磧中。葉如剪刀形。莖幹似嫩蒲，又似三棱。苗甚軟，其色深青綠。每叢十餘莖，內抽出一、兩莖，上分枝，開小白花，四瓣，蕊深黃色。根大者如杏，小者如栗，色白而瑩滑。五、六、七月採葉，正、二月採根，即慈菇也。煮熟味甘甜。

時珍曰：慈姑生淺水中，人亦種之。三月生苗，青莖中空，其外有棱。葉如燕尾，前尖後歧。霜後葉枯，根乃練結，冬及春初，掘以為果，須灰湯煮熟，去皮食，乃不麻澀戟人咽也。嫩莖亦可炸食。又取汁，可製粉霜、雌黃。又有山慈菇，名同實異，見草部。

根

【氣味】苦、甘，微寒，無毒。

大明曰：冷，有毒。多食，發虛熱，及腸風痔漏，崩中帶下，瘡癤。以生薑同煮佳。懷孕人不可食。詵曰：吳人常食之，令人發腳氣癱緩風，損齒失顏色，皮肉乾燥。猝食之，使人乾嘔也。

【主治】百毒，產後血悶，攻心欲死，產難胞衣不出，搗汁服一升。又下石淋（蘇恭）。

葉

諸惡瘡腫，小兒游瘤丹毒，搗爛塗之，即便消退，甚佳（蘇頌）。治蛇、蟲咬，搗爛封之（大明）。調蚌粉，塗之。
results.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate names: Jiégū [借姑] (Bielu), Shuǐpíng [水萍] (Bielu), Héfúcí [河鳧茈] (Tujing), Báidìlì [白地栗] (ibid.). The sprout is called Jiǎndāo cǎo [剪刀草, 'scissors grass']. The root produces twelve cormlets each year, like a mother arrowhead nursing its children — hence the name cígū [慈菇, lit. 'nurturing mushroom/corm']. The alternative spelling cígū [茨菰] is incorrect. The names Héfúcí and Báidìlì serve to distinguish this plant from the dark taro (wūyù) whose corms also carry the names fúcí and dìlì. Other folk names include: Jiǎndāo [剪刀, 'scissors'], Jiàndā [箭搭, 'arrow-nock'], Cháyā [槎丫, 'branched fork'], and Yànwěi [燕尾, 'swallow-tail'].

**[Collected Commentaries]**
The *Bielu* states: Jiégū — collect the roots on the third day of the third month and dry them in the sun.

Hongjing [Tao Hongjing] states: Jiégū grows in rice paddies. Its leaves are forked, resembling those of zéxiè [water plantain, *Alisma*]. The roots are yellow, smaller than taro corms, and can be eaten after boiling.

Gong [Su Gong] states: Cígū grows in water. Its leaves resemble arrowheads; it belongs to the same category as zéxiè [water plantain].

Song [Su Song] states: Jiǎndāo cǎo grows in rivers and lakes and in the sandy, gravelly channels near water around Bianliang and Luoyang. Its leaves are shaped like scissors. The stem resembles tender cattail reed, or also the three-edged sedge. The seedlings are very soft, deep blue-green in colour. Each clump has more than ten stems; one or two stems emerge from the centre and branch at the top, bearing small white four-petalled flowers with deep yellow stamens. The larger roots are the size of apricots, the smaller ones like chestnuts, white, glossy, and smooth. Leaves are gathered in the fifth, sixth, and seventh months; roots are gathered in the first and second months — these are the cígū. Boiled, the flavour is sweet.

Li Shizhen states: Cígū grows in shallow water and is also cultivated by people. Sprouts appear in the third month; the stems are blue-green, hollow inside, and ridged on the outside. The leaves resemble swallow-tails — pointed at the front and forked at the back. After frost the leaves wither and the roots consolidate. In winter and early spring they are dug up and used as food. They must be boiled in lye water [灰湯], then peeled before eating, otherwise they cause a numbing, astringent, throat-irritating sensation. The tender stems may also be fried and eaten. The juice can be used to prepare fěnshuāng [粉霜, calomel/mercury chloride] and cíhuáng [雌黃, orpiment/arsenic trisulphide]. There is also a 'mountain cígū' [山慈菇], which shares the name but is a different plant; see the Herbs section.

---
**Root**

**[Flavour and Nature]** Bitter and sweet; slightly cold; non-toxic.

Daming [Ri Huazi] states: [Its nature is] cold; [and it is] toxic. Consumed in excess, it generates vacuity-heat, intestinal wind, haemorrhoidal fistula [痔漏], flooding and spotting [崩中帶下], and sores and furuncles. It is best cooked together with fresh ginger. Pregnant women must not eat it.
Meng Shen [詵] states: The people of Wu eat it regularly, but it causes leg-qi [beriberi-like condition], flaccid wind-paralysis, damage to the teeth, loss of facial colour, and dryness of skin and flesh. Eating it suddenly causes dry retching.

**[Indications and Treatment]**
For the hundred poisons; post-partum blood oppression attacking the heart with near-fatal outcome; difficult labour with retained placenta — pound and take one sheng [升] of the juice orally. Also expels stone strangury [石淋] (Su Gong).

---
**Leaf**

For all manner of malignant sores and swellings, and wandering-tumour erysipelas [游瘤丹毒] in children — pound to a pulp and apply topically; the swelling retreats immediately; excellent (Su Song). For snake and insect bites — pound to a pulp and apply as a poultice (Daming). Mix with clam-shell powder [蚌粉] and apply.

entities_mentioned.name: 慈菇 (cígū) / 借姑 (jiégū) / 剪刀草 (jiǎndāo cǎo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sagittaria sagittifolia or Sagittaria trifolia (arrowhead, family Alismataceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of arrowhead-shaped leaves, aquatic habitat, white four-petalled flowers, and edible corms is consistent with Sagittaria trifolia (Chinese arrowhead), widely cultivated in East Asia. Li Shizhen carefully distinguishes this plant from 'mountain cígū' (山慈菇, likely Pleione or Iphigenia species) and from wūyù / black taro (Eleocharis dulcis or Trapa natans types).

entities_mentioned.name: 澤瀉 (zéxiè)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alisma plantago-aquatica (common water plantain)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used repeatedly as a morphological comparator for leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: 山慈菇 (shān cígū)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Tulipa edulis, Pleione bulbocodioides, or Iphigenia indica — cross-reference required in the Herbs section
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen explicitly notes this is a different plant sharing the same name and refers readers to the Herbs (草部) section.

entities_mentioned.name: 粉霜 (fěnshuāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calomel (mercurous chloride, Hg₂Cl₂)
entities_mentioned.notes: The text states cígū juice can be used in preparing fěnshuāng; the exact role is unclear — possibly as a reducing or carrier agent in alchemical processing.

entities_mentioned.name: 雌黃 (cíhuáng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orpiment (arsenic trisulphide, As₂S₃)
entities_mentioned.notes: A mineral pigment and medicinal/alchemical substance; the juice of cígū is said to assist in its preparation.

results.translator_notes: 1. The opening line about the sprout name appears to have been run together in the source OCR ('苗名剪刀草根歲生十二子'); the sentence break has been restored as '苗名剪刀草。根歲生十二子' in the cleaned text, which gives coherent meaning. 2. The tail of the text ('調蚌粉，') is cut off mid-sentence; it likely continues with instructions to apply the mixture topically for sores or insect bites. This has been rendered as completely as possible with a note of truncation. 3. 'Intestinal wind' (腸風), 'flooding and spotting' (崩中帶下), 'leg-qi' (腳氣), and 'flaccid wind' (癱緩風) are retained as period medical terminology with brief bracketed glosses. 4. 'Lye water' (灰湯, lit. ash water) refers to alkaline water made from plant-ash lye, used to neutralize the acrid compounds in the corms.

results.original_text:
（《拾遺》）

凡果未成核者，食之令人發癰癤及寒熱。

凡果落地有惡蟲緣過者，食之令人患九漏。

凡果雙仁者，有毒殺人。

凡瓜雙蒂者，有毒殺人；沉水者，殺人。

凡果忽有異常者，根下必有毒蛇，食之殺人。

互考楮實 梧桐子 枸杞子 金櫻子 山茱萸 桑椹 木半夏 胡頹子 松花 桂花 櫟實（已上木部） 黃覆盆子（已上草部）
results.translation:
（From the 《Shiyi》 [Supplements to Materia Medica, by Chen Cangqi]）

Any fruit that has not yet developed a pit [stone/kernel]: eating it causes the person to develop carbuncles and furuncles [yōng and jiē], as well as alternating chills and fever.

Any fruit that has fallen to the ground and been crawled over by noxious insects: eating it causes the person to suffer from the nine types of fistula [jiǔ lòu, a classical category of suppurative fistulous conditions].

Any fruit with a double kernel [two seeds within one stone]: it is toxic and will kill a person.

Any melon or gourd with a double stem [two joined stalks]: it is toxic and will kill a person. Likewise, any melon or gourd that sinks in water will kill a person.

Any fruit that suddenly appears abnormal [unusual in shape, color, or growth]: there is certainly a venomous snake beneath the roots of that plant, and eating the fruit will kill a person.

Cross-reference entries: Chǔ shí [paper mulberry fruit], Wútóng zǐ [Chinese parasol tree seeds], Gǒuqǐ zǐ [wolfberry], Jīnyīng zǐ [Cherokee rose fruit], Shān zhūyú [cornelian cherry], Sāng shèn [mulberry fruit], Mù bànxià [woody pinellia], Hú tuí zǐ [autumn olive/Elaeagnus fruit], Sōng huā [pine pollen], Guì huā [osmanthus flower], Lì shí [acorn/oak fruit] — (all listed under the Wood section); Huáng fúpén zǐ [yellow raspberry] — (listed under the Herb/Grass section).

entities_mentioned.name: 癰癤 (yōng jiē)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carbuncles and furuncles (bacterial skin abscesses)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical pathological category; corresponds broadly to suppurative skin infections.

entities_mentioned.name: 九漏 (jiǔ lòu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fistulous conditions (nine classical types of fistula/sinus tracts)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Chinese medical category covering nine varieties of suppurating fistulae or draining sinuses, often associated with scrofula or lymphadenopathy.

entities_mentioned.name: 楮實 (Chǔ shí)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Broussonetia papyrifera (paper mulberry) — fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Wood section.

entities_mentioned.name: 梧桐子 (Wútóng zǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Firmiana simplex (Chinese parasol tree) — seeds
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Wood section.

entities_mentioned.name: 枸杞子 (Gǒuqǐ zǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium barbarum or L. chinense (wolfberry) — fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Wood section.

entities_mentioned.name: 金櫻子 (Jīnyīng zǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rosa laevigata (Cherokee rose) — fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Wood section.

entities_mentioned.name: 山茱萸 (Shān zhūyú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis (cornelian cherry / Japanese cornel) — fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Wood section.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑椹 (Sāng shèn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba or M. rubra (mulberry) — fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Wood section.

entities_mentioned.name: 木半夏 (Mù bànxià)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Elaeagnus multiflora or related species — possibly 'woody pinellia'; sometimes identified as a species of Elaeagnus
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; identity debated. Name literally means 'woody pinellia' but is distinct from the herbaceous Pinellia ternata.

entities_mentioned.name: 胡頹子 (Hú tuí zǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Elaeagnus pungens or related Elaeagnus species (thorny olive / silverberry) — fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Wood section.

entities_mentioned.name: 松花 (Sōng huā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinus spp. — pine pollen
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Wood section.

entities_mentioned.name: 桂花 (Guì huā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Osmanthus fragrans — osmanthus flower
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Wood section.

entities_mentioned.name: 櫟實 (Lì shí)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quercus spp. — acorn / oak fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Wood section.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃覆盆子 (Huáng fúpén zǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubus spp. — yellow raspberry
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-reference only; treated under the Herb/Grass section. The yellow variety is distinguished from the common fúpén zǐ (R. chingii).

results.translator_notes: This segment is attributed to the Bencao Shiyi (《本草拾遺》, 'Supplements to the Materia Medica') by Chen Cangqi (Tang dynasty). The content is a series of general folk-medical rules about toxic fruits rather than a description of a single plant. The 'cross-reference' (互考) list at the end directs readers to other entries in the Bencao Gangmu where fruits and fruit-like products appear under the Wood (木部) and Herb/Grass (草部) sections rather than the Fruit section (果部). The rule about melon sinking in water is a well-known traditional toxicity test: normally ripe melons float; a sinking melon was considered abnormal and dangerous. The 'double kernel' rule reflects a broader traditional belief across many cultures that abnormal twinned seeds are inauspicious or poisonous. OCR appears reliable; no illegible passages detected.

results.original_text:
【釋名】草鐘乳（《拾遺》）、起陽草（侯氏《藥譜》）。

頌曰：案：許慎《說文》：韭字，象葉出地上形。一種而久生，故謂之韭。一歲三、四割，其根不傷，至冬壅培之，先春複生，信乎久生者也。

藏器曰：俗謂韭葉是草鐘乳，言其溫補也。

時珍曰：韭之莖名韭白，根名韭黃，花名韭菁。《禮記》謂韭為豐本，言其美在根也。薤之美在白，韭之美在黃，黃乃未出土者。

【集解】時珍曰：韭叢生豐本，長葉青翠。可以根分，可以子種。其性內生，不得外長。葉高三寸便剪，剪忌日中。一歲不過五剪，收子者只可一剪。八月開花成叢，收取腌藏供饌，謂之長生韭，言剪而複生，久而不乏也。九月收子，其子黑色而扁，須風處陰乾，勿令郁。北人至冬移根於土窖中，培以馬屎，暖則即長，高可尺許，不見風日，其葉黃嫩，謂之韭黃，豪貴皆珍之。韭之為菜，可生可熟，可菹可久，乃菜中最有益者也。羅願《爾雅翼》云：物久必變，故老韭為莧。

頌曰：鄭玄言政道得則陰物變為陽，故蔥變為韭，可驗蔥冷而韭溫也。

【氣味】辛、微酸，溫，澀，無毒。時珍曰：生：辛、澀；熟：甘、酸。大明曰：熱。

宗奭曰：春食則香，夏食則臭，多食則能昏神暗目，酒後尤忌。

詵曰：熱病後十日食之，

{
  "title_inferred": "Jiǔ (Garlic Chives / Chinese Chives) — Vegetables, Section One",
  "original_text": "【釋名】草鐘乳（《拾遺》）、起陽草（侯氏《藥譜》）。

頌曰：案：許慎《說文》：韭字，象葉出地上形。一種而久生，故謂之韭。一歲三、四割，其根不傷，至冬壅培之，先春複生，信乎久生者也。

藏器曰：俗謂韭葉是草鐘乳，言其溫補也。

時珍曰：韭之莖名韭白，根名韭黃，花名韭菁。《禮記》謂韭為豐本，言其美在根也。薤之美在白，韭之美在黃，黃乃未出土者。

【集解】時珍曰：韭叢生豐本，長葉青翠。可以根分，可以子種。其性內生，不得外長。葉高三寸便剪，剪忌日中。一歲不過五剪，收子者只可一剪。八月開花成叢，收取腌藏供饌，謂之長生韭，言剪而複生，久而不乏也。九月收子，其子黑色而扁，須風處陰乾，勿令郁。北人至冬移根於土窖中，培以馬屎，暖則即長，高可尺許，不見風日，其葉黃嫩，謂之韭黃，豪貴皆珍之。韭之為菜，可生可熟，可菹可久，乃菜中最有益者也。羅願《爾雅翼》云：物久必變，故老韭為莧。

頌曰：鄭玄言政道得則陰物變為陽，故蔥變為韭，可驗蔥冷而韭溫也。

【氣味】辛、微酸，溫，澀，無毒。時珍曰：生：辛、澀；熟：甘、酸。大明曰：熱。

宗奭曰：春食則香，夏食則臭，多食則能昏神暗目，酒後尤忌。

詵曰：熱病後十日食之，即發困。五月多食，乏氣力。冬月多食，動宿飲，吐水。不可與蜜及牛肉同食。
辛、甘，溫，無毒。

時珍曰：陽也。伏石鐘乳、乳香。

【主治】歸心，安五臟，除胃中熱，利病患，可久食（《別錄》。時珍曰：案：《千金方》作可久食，不利病患。）

葉：煮鯽魚酢食，斷卒下痢。根：入生發膏用（弘景）。

根、葉：煮食，溫中下氣，補虛益陽，調和臟腑，令人能食，止泄血膿，腹中冷痛。生搗汁服，主胸痺骨痛不可觸者，又解藥毒，療狂狗咬人數發者，亦塗諸蛇虺、蠍蠆、惡蟲毒（藏器）。煮食，充肺氣，除心腹痼冷癖。搗汁服，治肥白人中風失音（《日華》）。煮食，歸腎壯陽，止泄精，暖腰膝（寧原）。炸熟，以鹽、醋空心吃十頓，治胸膈噎氣。搗汁服，治胸痺刺痛如錐，即吐出胸中惡血甚驗。又灌初生小兒，吐去惡水、惡血，永無諸病（詵）。主吐血唾血，衄血尿血，婦人經脈逆行，打撲傷損及膈噎病。搗汁澄清，和童尿飲之，能消散胃脘瘀血，甚效（震亨）。飲生汁，主上氣喘息欲絕，解肉脯毒。煮汁飲，止消渴盜汗。熏產婦血運，洗腸痔脫肛（時珍）。
夢中泄精，溺白（《別錄》）。暖腰膝，治鬼交，甚效（《日華》）。補肝及命門，治小便頻數、遺尿，女人白淫、白帶（時珍）。

【發明】弘景曰：此菜殊辛臭，雖煮食之，便出猶熏灼，不如蔥、薤熟即無氣，最是養生所忌。

頌曰：菜中此物最溫而益人，宜常食之。昔人正月節食五辛以辟癘氣，謂韭、薤、蔥、蒜、薑也。

宗奭曰：韭黃未出糞土，最不益人，食之滯氣，蓋含抑郁未申之氣故也。孔子曰：「不時不食」，正謂此類。花食之亦動風。

思邈曰：韭味酸，肝病宜食之，大益人心。

時珍曰：韭，葉熱根溫，功用相同。生則辛而散血，熟則甘而補中。入足厥陰經，乃肝之菜也。《素問》言，心病宜食韭，《食鑒本草》言：歸腎，文雖異而理則相貫。蓋心乃肝之子，腎乃肝之母，母能令子實，虛則補其母也。道家目為五葷之一，謂其能昏人神而動虛陽也。有一貧叟病噎膈，食入即吐，胸中刺痛。或令取韭汁，入鹽、梅、鹵汁少許，細呷，得入漸加，忽吐稠涎數升而愈。此亦仲景治胸痺用薤白，皆取其辛溫能散胃脘痰飲惡血之義也。

震亨曰：心痛有食熱物及怒郁，致死血留於胃口作痛者，宜用韭汁、桔梗加入。",

Jin Youzhi's *Beizhengli* [Record of the Northern Expedition] states: 'In the frontier garrison lands around Yuntai in the north, wild chives [yejiu] and sand-onions [shacong] grow abundantly, and the people all gather and eat them.' This is the same plant. Whether Su's identification of the 'yu' in the Odes with this plant is correct remains uncertain. Furthermore, Lü Chen's *Zilin* states: 䪥 (pronounced *yan*) is the water chive [shuijiu], growing wild on water margins, with leaves like chive but slender and elongated, and edible. From this it is clear that wild chives comprise two varieties — mountain and water — whose flavour and properties are probably not greatly different.

【釋名】芤（《綱目》）、菜伯（同）、和事草（同）、鹿胎。

時珍曰：蔥從囪。外直中空，有囪通之象也。芤者，草中有孔也，故字從孔，芤脈象之。蔥初生曰蔥針，葉曰蔥青，衣曰蔥袍，莖曰蔥白，葉中涕曰蔥苒。諸物皆宜，故云菜伯、和事。

【集解】恭曰：蔥有數種，山蔥曰蔥，療病似胡蔥。其人間食蔥有二種：一種凍蔥，經冬不死，分莖栽蒔而無子；一種漢蔥，冬即葉枯。食用入藥，凍蔥最善，氣味亦佳也。

保升曰：蔥凡四種：冬蔥即凍蔥也，夏衰冬盛，莖葉俱軟美，山南、江左有之；漢蔥莖實硬而味薄，冬即葉枯；胡蔥莖葉粗短，根若金燈；蔥生於山谷，不入藥用。

頌曰：入藥用山蔥、胡蔥，食品用冬蔥、漢蔥。又有一種樓蔥，亦冬蔥類，江南人呼為龍角蔥，荊楚間多種之，其皮赤，每莖上出歧如八角，故云。

瑞曰：龍角即龍爪蔥，又名羊角蔥。莖上生根，移下蒔之。

時珍曰：冬蔥即慈蔥，或名太官蔥。謂其莖柔細而香，可以經冬，太官上供宜之，故有數名。漢蔥一名木蔥，其莖粗硬，故有木名。冬蔥無子。漢蔥春末開花成叢，青白色。其子味辛色黑，有皺紋，作三瓣狀。收取陰乾，勿令鬱，可種可栽。
results.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Alternate names: Kou [芤] (Bencao Gangmu), Caibo [菜伯, 'Elder of Vegetables'], Heshicao [和事草, 'Harmony Herb'], and Lutai [鹿胎, 'Deer's Fetus'].

Li Shizhen states: The character 蔥 (cong) derives from 囪 (cong, chimney/hollow). The plant is straight on the outside and hollow within, resembling the image of a chimney passage. Kou [芤] means 'a hollow in a plant,' hence the character incorporates the element for 'hole' (孔); the kou pulse [芤脈, a hollow pulse in Chinese medicine] takes its image from this. The first sprouts of the plant are called 'cong needles' [蔥針]; the leaves are called 'cong green' [蔥青]; the outer sheath is called 'cong robe' [蔥袍]; the stalk is called 'cong white' [蔥白]; and the mucilaginous fluid inside the leaves is called 'cong ran' [蔥苒]. Because it harmonizes with all foods, it is called 'Elder of Vegetables' and 'Harmony Herb.'

**Collected Commentaries**
[Su] Gong states: There are several varieties of cong. The mountain cong [山蔥] is a type of cong; its medicinal use resembles that of the hu-cong [胡蔥, foreign/Central Asian onion]. Among the varieties eaten by common people there are two kinds: one is the frozen cong [凍蔥], which does not die through winter, is propagated by dividing the stem and transplanting, and produces no seeds; the other is the Han cong [漢蔥], whose leaves wither in winter. For both eating and medicinal use, the frozen cong is the finest, with excellent aroma and flavor.

[Han] Baosheng states: There are altogether four varieties of cong. Winter cong [冬蔥] is the same as frozen cong: it is sparse in summer and thrives in winter, with stems and leaves that are both tender and delicious; it is found in Shannan and Jiangzuo. Han cong has a firm, hard stalk and a thin flavor, and its leaves wither in winter. Hu-cong has coarse, short stems and leaves, with roots resembling golden lantern bulbs [金燈, likely referring to the bulb shape]. Mountain cong grows in mountain valleys and is not used medicinally.

[Su] Song states: Mountain cong and hu-cong are used medicinally, while winter cong and Han cong are used as food. There is also a variety called lou-cong [樓蔥, 'tower onion'], which belongs to the winter cong type. People in Jiangnan call it 'dragon-horn cong' [龍角蔥]; it is widely cultivated in the Jing-Chu region. Its skin is red, and from each stalk grow branches resembling eight-pointed angles, hence the name.

[Wu] Rui states: Dragon-horn is the same as dragon-claw cong [龍爪蔥], also called sheep-horn cong [羊角蔥]. Roots grow from the stalk, which are then removed and transplanted.

Li Shizhen states: Winter cong is the same as ci-cong [慈蔥, 'mild/gracious onion'], also known as Taiguan cong [太官蔥, 'Imperial Provisioner's onion']. Its stalk is slender, soft, and fragrant, and it can survive the winter; it is well suited for imperial provisioning, hence the various names. Han cong is also called mu-cong [木蔥, 'wood onion'], because its stalk is coarse and hard, hence the name 'wood.' Winter cong produces no seeds. Han cong blooms in clusters at the end of spring, with blue-white flowers. Its seeds are pungent in flavor, black in color, wrinkled, and trilobate in form. They should be collected and dried in the shade, without being allowed to become stuffy or damp; they may then be sown or planted.

entities_mentioned.name: 蔥 (Cong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium fistulosum L. (Welsh onion / bunching onion)
entities_mentioned.notes: The general category 蔥 corresponds broadly to Allium fistulosum, the most common East Asian bunching/Welsh onion. Multiple cultivar-type distinctions are described below.

entities_mentioned.name: 凍蔥 / 冬蔥 / 慈蔥 / 太官蔥 (Dong-cong / Winter cong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium fistulosum var. caespitosum (or a related perennial bunching type)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as perennial, propagated vegetatively, no seeds, tender and fragrant stalk; possibly corresponds to a perennial clumping form of A. fistulosum.

entities_mentioned.name: 漢蔥 / 木蔥 (Han-cong / Mu-cong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium fistulosum var. giganteum or a related coarser-stalked cultivar
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having a hard, firm stalk and thin flavor; leaves wither in winter; produces black, wrinkled, trilobate seeds. Likely a winter-deciduous cultivar of A. fistulosum.

entities_mentioned.name: 胡蔥 (Hu-cong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium ascalonicum L. (shallot) or Allium cepa var. aggregatum
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having coarse, short stems and leaves, with roots resembling golden lantern bulbs; likely a shallot or aggregatum-type onion of Central Asian origin.

entities_mentioned.name: 山蔥 (Shan-cong / Mountain cong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium victorialis L. or related wild mountain Allium species
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as growing in mountain valleys; used medicinally but not as food. Could be one of several wild Allium species found in Chinese highlands.

entities_mentioned.name: 樓蔥 / 龍角蔥 / 龍爪蔥 / 羊角蔥 (Lou-cong / Dragon-horn cong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium fistulosum var. viviparum (top-setting or tree onion)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having red skin, branches forking from each stalk like eight angles, and propagated by bulbils or aerial roots; consistent with viviparous/top-setting onion forms.

results.translator_notes: 1. Several character names in the original appear to be OCR artifacts where the specific variant of 蔥 (e.g., 山蔥 type names) was lost; the sense is preserved contextually. 2. The term 芤脈 (kou pulse) is a classical Chinese medical pulse description denoting a hollow pulse felt under finger pressure, likened to the hollow stalk of the cong plant. 3. '金燈' (golden lantern) in the description of hu-cong roots likely refers to the lantern-shaped bulb of the shallot. 4. One instance of a likely OCR gap ('勿令 郁') has been silently restored to '勿令鬱' (do not allow to become damp/stuffy), which is the standard reading in this context.

【氣味】辛，平。葉：溫。根須：平。並無毒。

弘景曰：蔥有寒熱，白冷青熱，傷寒湯中不得用青也。

宗奭曰：蔥主發散，多食昏人神。

詵曰：蔥宜冬月食。不可過多，損鬚髮，發人虛氣上沖，五臟閉絕，為其開骨節出汗之故也。

思邈曰：正月食生蔥，令人面上起游風。生蔥同蜜食，作下利。燒蔥同蜜食，壅氣殺人。

張仲景曰：生蔥合棗食，令人病；合犬、雉肉食，多令人病血。

時珍曰：服地黃、常山人，忌食蔥。
辛，溫，滑，無毒
辛，大溫，無毒。

【主治】作湯，治傷寒寒熱，中風面目浮腫，能出汗（《本經》）。傷寒骨肉碎痛，喉痺
不通，安胎，歸目益目睛，除肝中邪氣，安中利五臟，殺百藥毒。根：治傷寒頭痛（《別錄
》）。主天行時疾，頭痛熱狂，霍亂轉筋，及奔豚氣、腳氣，心腹痛，目眩，止心迷悶（大明）。通關節，止衄血，利大小便（孟詵）。治陽明下痢、下血（李杲）。達表和裡，止血（寧
原）。除風濕，身痛麻痺，蟲積心痛，止大人陽脫，陰毒腹痛，小兒盤腸內釣，婦人妊娠溺血，通乳汁，散乳癰，利耳鳴，塗 犬傷，製蚯蚓毒（時珍）。

殺一切魚、肉毒（士良）。
煨研，敷金瘡水入皸腫。鹽研，敷蛇、蟲傷及中射工、溪毒（《日華》）。

時珍曰：服地黃、常山人，忌食蔥。
辛，溫，滑，無毒
辛，大溫，無毒。

【主治】作湯，治傷寒寒熱，中風面目浮腫，能出汗（《本經》）。傷寒骨肉碎痛，喉痺不通，安胎，歸目益目睛，除肝中邪氣，安中利五臟，殺百藥毒。根：治傷寒頭痛（《別錄》）。主天行時疾，頭痛熱狂，霍亂轉筋，及奔豚氣、腳氣，心腹痛，目眩，止心迷悶（大明）。通關節，止衄血，利大小便（孟詵）。治陽明下痢、下血（李杲）。達表和裡，止血（寧原）。除風濕，身痛麻痺，蟲積心痛，止大人陽脫，陰毒腹痛，小兒盤腸內釣，婦人妊娠溺血，通乳汁，散乳癰，利耳鳴，塗犬傷，製蚯蚓毒（時珍）。

殺一切魚、肉毒（士良）。
煨研，敷金瘡水入皸腫。鹽研，敷蛇、蟲傷及中射工、溪毒（《日華》）。

主水病足腫（蘇頌）。利五臟，益目精，發黃膽（思邈）。
溺血，飲之。解藜蘆及桂毒（《別錄》）。散瘀血，止衄止痛，治頭痛耳聾，消痔漏，解眾藥毒（時珍）。能消桂為水，化五石，仙方所用（弘景）。
通氣（孟詵）。療飽食房勞，血滲入大腸，便血腸澼成痔，晒乾，研末，每服二錢，溫酒下（時珍）。
心脾痛如錐刀刺，腹脹。用一升，同吳茱萸一升，水一大升八合，煎七合，去滓，分三服，立效（頌，出崔元亮方）。

實
明目，補中氣不足（《本經》）。溫中益精（《日華》）。宜肺，歸頭（思邈）。

【發明】元素曰：蔥莖白，味辛而甘平，氣濃味薄，升也，陽也。入手太陰、足陽明經，專主發散，以通上下陽氣。故《活人書》治傷寒頭痛如破，用連鬚蔥白湯主之。張仲景治少陰病，下利清谷，裡寒外熱，厥逆脈微者，白通湯主之，內用蔥白。若面色赤者，四逆湯加蔥白。腹中痛者，去蔥白。成無己解之云：腎惡燥，急食辛以潤之。蔥白辛溫以通陽氣也。

時珍曰：蔥乃釋家五葷之一。生辛散，熟甘溫，外實中空，肺之菜也，肺病宜食之。肺主氣，外應皮毛，其合陽明。故所治之症多屬太陰、陽明，皆取其發散通氣之功，通氣故能解毒及理血病。氣者血之帥也，氣通則血活矣。金瘡磕損，折傷血出，疼痛不止者，王《百一選方》用蔥白、砂糖等分研封之。云痛立止，更無痕瘢也。蔥葉亦可用。又蔥管吹鹽入玉莖內，治小便不通及轉脬危急者，極有捷效。余常用治數人得驗。

results.title_inferred: Cong (Mountain Onion / Wild Onion) — Vegetables, Part One
results.original_text:
【釋名】山蔥。

【集解】保升曰：蔥生山谷，不入藥用。

頌曰：《爾雅》云：，山蔥也。《郭注》云：蔥生山中，細莖大葉。食之香美於常蔥，宜入藥用。

時珍曰：蔥，野蔥也，山原平地皆有之。生沙地者名沙蔥，生水澤者名水蔥，野人皆食之。開白花，結子如小蔥頭。世俗不察胡蔥即蒜蔥，誤指此為胡蔥（詳見胡蔥下）。保升言不入藥用，蘇頌言入藥宜用山蔥、胡蔥。今考思邈《千金食性》，自有蔥功用，而諸本失收，今采補之。

【氣味】辛，微溫，無毒。

時珍曰：佛家以蔥為五葷之一（見蒜下）。同蔥。

【主治】除瘴氣惡毒。久食，強志益膽氣（思邈）。主諸惡、狐尿刺毒，山溪中沙虱、射工等毒。煮汁浸，或搗敷，大效。亦兼小蒜、茱萸輩，不獨用也（蘇恭）。

子：泄精（思邈）。
results.translation:
**Explanatory Names:** Mountain onion [shān cōng].

**Collected Commentaries:**
Kai Baosheng states: This onion grows in mountain valleys and is not used medicinally.

Su Song states: The *Erya* says: [this character] is the mountain onion. Guo Pu's commentary notes: this onion grows in the mountains, with slender stems and large leaves. In flavor and fragrance it surpasses the common onion, and it is suitable for medicinal use.

Li Shizhen states: This [cōng] is the wild onion; it grows everywhere, on mountain plains and flatlands alike. That which grows in sandy soil is called the sand onion [shā cōng]; that which grows in marshes and wetlands is called the water onion [shuǐ cōng]. Rural folk eat all of these. It bears white flowers and forms seeds resembling small onion bulbs. Common people, failing to distinguish that the hu onion [hú cōng] is in fact the garlic-onion [suàn cōng], have mistakenly identified this plant as the hu onion (see the entry on hu onion below for details). Baosheng said it is not used medicinally; Su Song said it is the mountain onion and hu onion that are suitable for medicinal use. Having now consulted Sun Simiao's *Qianjin Shixing* [Dietary Properties from the Thousand Gold Formulae], I find that the properties and uses of this onion are indeed recorded there, though omitted by all other editions; I therefore supplement and include them here.

**Flavor and Nature:** Acrid [xīn], slightly warm, non-toxic.

Li Shizhen states: Buddhists regard this onion as one of the Five Pungent Roots [wǔ hūn] (see the entry on garlic). [Its properties are] the same as common onion.

**Indications and Treatments:**
Eliminates miasmic [zhàng] qi and malignant toxins. Eaten over a long period, it strengthens the will and benefits the gallbladder qi (Sun Simiao). It governs all manner of malignant [sores/ailments], the toxic sting of fox-urine [hú niào cì dú], and the toxins of sand-lice [shā shī] and archer-worms [shè gōng] found in mountain streams. Boil [the plant] into a decoction for soaking [the affected area], or pound it and apply as a poultice — both methods are highly effective. It is also used in combination with small garlic [xiǎo suàn] and cornelian dogwood [zhū yú], not applied alone (Su Gong).

**Seeds:**
Cause seminal discharge [xiè jīng] (Sun Simiao).

entities_mentioned.name: 蔥 / 山蔥 (shān cōng, mountain onion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium victorialis or Allium macrostemon (wild mountain onion species native to East Asia)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen distinguishes this wild onion from the cultivated common onion and from the hu onion (胡蔥). The sand-onion and water-onion variants are likely ecotypes of the same or related Allium species.

entities_mentioned.name: 沙蔥 (shā cōng, sand onion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium mongolicum or similar sandy-habitat Allium species
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a variety growing in sandy soil.

entities_mentioned.name: 水蔥 (shuǐ cōng, water onion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium schoenoprasum or a wetland Allium species; note: the name 水蔥 is also applied to Schoenoplectus tabernaemontani (bulrush) in other contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as growing in marshes and wetlands.

entities_mentioned.name: 胡蔥 (hú cōng, hu onion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium ascalonicum (shallot) or Allium fistulosum var., sometimes identified as 蒜蔥
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen notes a common confusion between this wild onion and the hu onion; directs readers to a separate entry.

entities_mentioned.name: 小蒜 (xiǎo suàn, small garlic)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium scorodoprasum or Allium macrostemon
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a companion herb in topical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: 茱萸 (zhū yú, cornelian dogwood / evodia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tetradium ruticarpum (Wu Zhu Yu) or Cornus officinalis (Shan Zhu Yu); context suggests the former
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a companion herb alongside small garlic in the treatment of toxic stings and bites.

entities_mentioned.name: 沙虱 (shā shī, sand-lice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trombiculid mite larvae (chiggers); associated with scrub typhus vector in classical Chinese medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: A parasitic arthropod mentioned as a source of toxic envenomation.

entities_mentioned.name: 射工 (shè gōng, archer-worm / water-arrow insect)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Toxorhynchites or a mythologized creature; traditionally a water-dwelling insect believed to shoot toxic 'arrows'
entities_mentioned.notes: A legendary or semi-legendary toxic creature from mountain streams in classical Chinese medical literature.

results.translator_notes: 1. The entry heading character(s) for the plant name (蔥 here standing for 薤蔥 or a specific character) appear to have been partially lost or garbled in the OCR source — the *Erya* citation begins with a blank before 'mountain onion,' suggesting a specific character (likely 䕗 or 茖) was not captured. This has been silently noted but left as a gap. 2. 'Fox-urine sting toxin' (狐尿刺毒) refers to a classical category of toxic skin condition, possibly contact dermatitis or an insect/plant sting, not literally fox urine. 3. Sun Simiao's *Qianjin Shixing* (千金食性) is part of the *Beiji Qianjin Yaofang* corpus. 4. The seed entry noting 'seminal discharge' (泄精) as a use is ambiguous — it could indicate use as a treatment for seminal leakage or, conversely, as a substance that induces it; context from Sun Simiao suggests the former is more likely but the text is terse.

results.title_inferred: Hu Cong (Shallot / Garlic-Onion): Names, Description, Properties, and Uses
results.original_text:
【釋名】蒜蔥（《綱目》）、回回蔥。

時珍曰：按：《孫真人食忌》作葫蔥，因其根似葫蒜故也。俗稱蒜蔥，正合此義。元人《飲膳正要》作回回蔥，似言其來自胡地，故曰胡蔥耳。

【集解】志曰：胡蔥生蜀郡山谷。狀似大蒜而小，形圓皮赤，梢長而銳。五月、六月採。

保升曰：蔥凡四種：冬蔥夏枯；漢蔥冬枯；胡蔥莖葉粗短，根若金燈；蔥生於山谷。

頌曰：胡蔥類食蔥，而根莖皆細白。或云：莖葉微短如金燈。或云：似大蒜而小，形圓皮赤，梢長而銳。

時珍曰：胡蔥即蒜蔥也，馬志、韓保升所說是矣，非野蔥也。野蔥名蔥，似蔥而小。胡蔥乃人種蒔，八月下種，五月收取，葉似蔥而根似蒜，其味如薤，不甚臭。江西有水晶蔥，蒜根蔥葉，蓋其類也。李鵬飛《延壽書》言胡蔥即子，蓋因相似而誤爾。今俗皆以野蔥為胡蔥，因不識蒜蔥，故指蔥為之，謬矣。

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

時珍曰：生則辛平，熟則甘溫。

詵曰：亦是薰物。久食，傷神損性，令人多忘，損目明，絕血脈，發痼疾。患胡臭、齒人，食之轉甚。

思邈曰：四月勿食胡蔥，令人氣喘多驚。

【主治】溫中下氣，消穀能食，殺蟲，利五臟不足氣（孟詵）。

療腫毒（保升）。
中諸肉毒，吐血不止，萎黃悴者，以一升，水煮，冷服半升，日一夜一，血定乃止（孟詵）。

【發明】時珍曰：方術煮溪澗白石為糧，及煮牛、馬、驢骨令軟，皆用胡蔥，則亦軟堅之物也。陶弘景言蔥能化五石，消桂為水，則是諸蔥皆能軟石。故今人采蔥煮石，謂之胡蔥也。
results.translation:
**[Names]** Suancong [Garlic-Onion] (Bencao Gangmu); Huihuicong [Muslim Onion].

Li Shizhen states: According to Sun Simiao's *Shiji* [Dietary Prohibitions], it is written as Hucong [Gourd-Onion], because its root resembles the gourd-garlic [i.e., garlic bulb]. The common name Suancong [Garlic-Onion] matches this meaning precisely. The Yuan-dynasty work *Yinshan Zhengyao* [Proper and Essential Things for the Emperor's Food and Drink] records it as Huihuicong [Muslim Onion], seemingly indicating that it originated from the lands of the Hu [northwestern/Central Asian peoples]; hence the name Hucong [Hu Onion].

**[Collected Explanations]** Ma Zhi states: Hucong grows in the mountain valleys of Shu Commandery [modern Sichuan]. In form it resembles garlic but is smaller; its shape is round, its skin reddish, and its tip long and pointed. It is harvested in the fifth and sixth months.

Han Baosheng states: There are in all four kinds of cong [onion-type plants]: the Winter Onion [donggcong] withers in summer; the Han Onion [hancong] withers in winter; Hucong has thick, short stems and leaves, with roots resembling a golden lantern [Lycoris radiata bulb]; and the [wild] cong grows in mountain valleys.

Su Song states: Hucong is similar to the edible onion [shicong], but both root and stem are slender and white. Some say its stems and leaves are somewhat short, like a golden lantern [plant]. Others say it resembles garlic but is smaller, round in shape, with reddish skin, and a long, pointed tip.

Li Shizhen states: Hucong is precisely the Suancong [garlic-onion]. The accounts of Ma Zhi and Han Baosheng are correct; it is not the wild onion [yecong]. The wild onion is called [ye]cong and resembles the common onion but is smaller. Hucong is a cultivated plant: seeds are sown in the eighth month and it is harvested in the fifth month. Its leaves resemble those of the onion and its root resembles garlic; its flavor is like that of Chinese chive [xie, Allium chinense], and it is not strongly pungent. In Jiangxi there is a variety called Shuijingcong [Crystal Onion], with garlic-like roots and onion-like leaves — this is likely of the same type. Li Pengfei's *Yanshou Shu* [Book of Prolonging Life] claims that Hucong is identical to [another plant species — name illegible in source], but this is an error due to superficial resemblance. Nowadays people commonly take the wild onion for Hucong because they do not recognize Suancong, and so they mistakenly call the wild cong by this name — which is wrong.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Acrid [xin], warm [wen], non-toxic.

Li Shizhen states: When raw it is acrid and balanced [ping]; when cooked it is sweet and warm.

Meng Shen states: It is also a pungent-smelling [xun] substance. Prolonged consumption damages the spirit and impairs the nature; it causes forgetfulness, weakens eyesight, obstructs the blood vessels, and aggravates chronic diseases [guji]. Those suffering from body odor [huchou] or dental ailments will find their conditions worsen upon eating it.

Sun Simiao states: In the fourth month one should not eat Hucong, as it causes shortness of breath and frequent fright.

**[Indications and Treatment]**
- Warms the middle [jiao], descends qi, aids the digestion of grain and stimulates appetite, kills worms, and benefits the insufficient qi of the five viscera. (Meng Shen)
- Treats swelling and toxic sores [zhongdu]. (Han Baosheng)
- For poisoning from various meats, with incessant vomiting of blood, and emaciated, sallow, withered appearance: take one sheng [unit of volume] of Hucong, boil in water, allow to cool, and take half a sheng [per dose], once during the day and once at night, until the bleeding stops. (Meng Shen)

**[Elucidation]** Li Shizhen states: Alchemical arts that involve boiling white river stones for use as food, and boiling the bones of cattle, horses, or donkeys until soft, all make use of Hucong — demonstrating that it is a substance capable of softening hard things. Tao Hongjing noted that cong [onions] can dissolve the Five Minerals [wushi] and reduce cinnamon [gui] to liquid form, indicating that all varieties of cong can soften stone. Hence people today gather [wild] cong to boil stones, calling this method 'Hucong [cooking].'

entities_mentioned.name: 胡蔥 (Hucong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium ascalonicum (shallot) or possibly Allium chinense / Allium fistulosum variant; the garlic-rooted, onion-leaved description strongly suggests shallot
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen distinguishes this cultivated plant from the wild onion (野蔥). The description — garlic-like bulb, onion-like leaves, flavor resembling xie (Allium chinense) — is most consistent with the shallot (Allium ascalonicum). The name 'Huihuicong' (Muslim Onion) suggests a Central Asian origin, consistent with the shallot's historical distribution.

entities_mentioned.name: 蒜蔥 (Suancong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium ascalonicum (shallot) — alternate name for 胡蔥
entities_mentioned.notes: Recorded as a synonym in the Bencao Gangmu itself.

entities_mentioned.name: 回回蔥 (Huihuicong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium ascalonicum or related Central Asian allium
entities_mentioned.notes: Yuan-dynasty term recorded in Yinshan Zhengyao; 'Huihui' refers to Muslim/Central Asian peoples, implying a western import.

entities_mentioned.name: 水晶蔥 (Shuijingcong, Crystal Onion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a local Jiangxi variety of shallot or Allium chinense
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having garlic-like roots and onion-like leaves; Li Shizhen considers it related to Hucong.

entities_mentioned.name: 金燈 (Jindeng, Golden Lantern)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycoris radiata (red spider lily) — used as a reference for bulb shape
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the root shape of Hucong.

entities_mentioned.name: 薤 (Xie)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium chinense (Chinese chive / rakkyo)
entities_mentioned.notes: Flavor of Hucong is compared to xie.

results.translator_notes:
1. Several plant names in the source text appear to be garbled or partially illegible (likely OCR/transcription issues), particularly the repeated unnamed 'cong' types in Han Baosheng's fourfold classification and the plant name in Li Pengfei's claim. These have been rendered with contextual placeholders or noted as illegible where identification is impossible.
2. The 'Five Minerals' (五石, wushi) refer to a classical set of mineral drugs (e.g., cinnabar, realgar, orpiment, magnetite, malachite) used in Daoist alchemical and medicinal practice.
3. Meng Shen's dosage: 一升 (yi sheng) is a classical volume measure, approximately 200–300 mL in Tang-era standards.
4. The conditions 'huichou' (胡臭) and 'chi ren' (齒人) likely refer to axillary bromhidrosis (body odor) and persons with dental disease or tooth decay, respectively.
5. The passage about boiling stones reflects Daoist alchemical traditions and should not be read as literal dietary practice.

【釋名】藠子（音叫，或作蕎者非）、莜子（音釣）、火蔥（《綱目》）、菜芝（《別錄》）、鴻薈（音會）。

時珍曰：薤本文作𧐆，韭類也。故字從韭，從㕢（音概），諧聲也。今人因其根白，呼為藠子，江南人訛為莜子。其葉類蔥而根如蒜，收種宜火熏，故俗人稱為火蔥。羅願云：物莫美於芝，故薤為菜芝。蘇頌復附莜子於蒜條，誤矣。

【集解】《別錄》曰：薤生魯山平澤。

恭曰：薤是韭類。葉似韭而闊，多白而無實。有赤、白二種：白者補而美，赤者苦而無味。

頌曰：薤處處有之。春秋分蒔，至冬葉枯。《爾雅》云：葝，山薤也。生山中，莖葉與家薤相類，而根差長，葉差大，僅若鹿蔥，體性亦與家薤同。今人少用。

宗奭曰：薤葉如金燈葉，差狹而更光。故古人言薤露者，以其光滑難佇之義。

時珍曰：薤八月栽根，正月分蒔，宜肥壤。數枝一本，則茂而根大。葉狀似韭。韭葉中實而扁，有劍脊。薤葉中空，似細蔥葉而有稜，氣亦如蔥。二月開細花，紫白色。根如小蒜，一本數顆，相依而生。五月葉青則掘之，否則肉不滿也。其根煮食、芼酒、糟藏、醋浸皆宜。故《內則》云：切蔥、薤實諸醯以柔之。白樂天詩云，酥暖薤白酒，謂以酥炒薤白投酒中也。一種水晶蔥，蔥葉蒜根，與薤相似

  "title_inferred": "Xiè (薤) — Chinese Scallion / Rakkyo: Names, Description, and Medicinal Uses",
  "original_text": "【釋名】藠子（音叫，或作蕎者非）、莜子（音釣）、火蔥（《綱目》）、菜芝（《別錄》）、鴻薈（音會）。

時珍曰：薤本文作𧐆，韭類也。故字從韭，從㕢（音概），諧聲也。今人因其根白，呼為藠子，江南人訛為莜子。其葉類蔥而根如蒜，收種宜火熏，故俗人稱為火蔥。羅願云：物莫美於芝，故薤為菜芝。蘇頌復附莜子於蒜條，誤矣。

【集解】《別錄》曰：薤生魯山平澤。

恭曰：薤是韭類。葉似韭而闊，多白而無實。有赤、白二種：白者補而美，赤者苦而無味。

頌曰：薤處處有之。春秋分蒔，至冬葉枯。《爾雅》云：葝，山薤也。生山中，莖葉與家薤相類，而根差長，葉差大，僅若鹿蔥，體性亦與家薤同。今人少用。

宗奭曰：薤葉如金燈葉，差狹而更光。故古人言薤露者，以其光滑難佇之義。

時珍曰：薤八月栽根，正月分蒔，宜肥壤。數枝一本，則茂而根大。葉狀似韭。韭葉中實而扁，有劍脊。薤葉中空，似細蔥葉而有稜，氣亦如蔥。二月開細花，紫白色。根如小蒜，一本數顆，相依而生。五月葉青則掘之，否則肉不滿也。其根煮食、芼酒、糟藏、醋浸皆宜。故《內則》云：切蔥、薤實諸醯以柔之。白樂天詩云，酥暖薤白酒，謂以酥炒薤白投酒中也。一種水晶蔥，蔥葉蒜根，與薤相似，不臭，亦其類也。按王禎《農書》云：野薤俗名天薤。生麥原中，葉似薤而小，味益辛，亦可供食，但不多有。即《爾雅》山薤是也。

薤白

【氣味】辛、苦，溫，滑，無毒。好古曰：入手陽明經。頌曰：薤宜去青留白，白冷而青熱也。詵曰：發熱病，不宜多食。三、四月勿食生者。大明曰：生食引涕唾。不可與牛肉同食，令人作症瘕。

【主治】金瘡、瘡敗。輕身，不飢，耐老（《本經》）。歸骨，除寒熱，去水氣，溫中，散結氣。作羹食，利病人。諸瘡、中風寒、水氣腫痛，搗塗之（《別錄》）。煮食，耐寒，調中補不足，止久痢冷瀉，肥健人（《日華》）。治泄痢下重，能泄下焦陽明氣滯（李杲。好古曰：下重者，氣滯也。四逆散加此以泄氣滯）。治少陰病厥逆泄痢，及胸痹刺痛，下氣散血，安胎（時珍）。心病宜食之。利產婦（思邈）。治女人帶下赤白，作羹食之。骨哽在咽不去者，食之即下（孟詵）。補虛解毒（蘇頌）。白者補益，赤者療金瘡及風，生肌肉（蘇恭）。與蜜同搗，塗湯火傷，效甚速（宗奭）。溫補，助陽道（時珍）。

【發明】弘景曰：薤性溫補，仙方及服食家皆須之，偏入諸膏用。不可生啖，葷辛為忌。

詵曰：薤，白色者最好，雖有辛，不葷五臟。學道人長服之，可通神安魂魄，益氣續筋力。

頌曰：白薤之白，性冷而補。又曰：莜子，煮與蓐婦飲，易產。亦主腳氣。

時珍曰：薤，味辛氣溫。諸家言其溫補，而蘇頌《圖經》獨謂其冷補。按杜甫《薤詩》云：束比青芻色，圓齊玉箸頭。衰年關膈冷，味暖並無憂。亦言其溫補，與經文相合。則冷補之說，蓋不然也。又按王禎云：薤生則氣辛，熟則甘美。種之不蠹，食之有益。故學道人資之，老人宜之。然道家以薤為五葷之一，而諸氏言其不葷何耶？薛用弱《齊諧志》云：安陸郭坦兄，得天行病後，遂能大餐，每日食至一斛。五年，家貧行乞。一日大飢，至一園，食薤一畦，大蒜一畦。便悶極臥地，吐一物如龍，漸漸縮小。有人撮飯於上，即消成水，而病尋瘳也。按此亦薤散結、蒜消症之驗也。

宗奭曰：薤葉光滑，露亦難佇。《千金》治肺氣喘急方中用之。",
  "translation": "**[Nomenclature]** Jiào zǐ [藠子] (pronounced jiào; the variant written as 蕎 is incorrect); Diào zǐ [莜子] (pronounced diào); Huǒ cōng [火蔥, 'fire onion'] (from the Bencao Gangmu); Cài zhī [菜芝, 'vegetable fungus-of-immortality'] (from the Bielu); Hóng huì [鴻薈] (pronounced huì).

Li Shizhen says: The character 薤 was originally written 𧐆 and belongs to the jiǔ [韭, chive] category. Hence the character is formed from 韭 and 㕢 (pronounced gài) as a phonetic component. People today, because its bulb i

於西域，遂呼此為小蒜以別之。故崔豹《古今注》云：蒜，茆蒜也，俗謂之小蒜。胡國有蒜，十子一株，名曰胡蒜，俗謂之大蒜是矣。蒜乃五葷之一，故許氏《說文》謂之葷菜。五葷即五辛，謂其辛臭昏神伐性也。練形家以小蒜、大蒜、韭、芸苔、胡荽為五葷，道家以韭、薤、蒜、芸苔、胡荽為五葷，佛家以大蒜、小蒜、興渠、慈蔥、 蔥為五葷。興渠，即阿魏也。

  "original_text": "【釋名】小蒜（《別錄》）、茆蒜（音卯）、葷菜。

雖各不同，然皆辛熏之物，生食增恚，熟食發淫，有損性靈，故絕之也。

【集解】《別錄》曰：蒜，小蒜也。五月五日采之。

弘景曰：小蒜生葉時，可煮和食。至五月葉枯，取根名子，正爾啖之，亦甚熏臭。

保升曰：小蒜野生，處處有之。小者一名（音亂），一名（音力）。苗、葉、根、子皆似葫，而細數倍也。《爾雅》云：山蒜也。《說文》云：蒜，葷菜也。菜之美者，云夢之葷菜。生山中者，名。

頌曰：本草謂大蒜為葫，小蒜為蒜，而《說文》所謂葷菜者，乃大蒜也，即小蒜也。書傳載物之別名不同如此，用藥不可不審。

宗奭曰：小蒜即也。苗如蔥針，根白，大者如烏芋子。兼根煮食，謂之宅蒜。

時珍曰：家蒜有二種：根莖俱小而瓣少，辣甚者，蒜也，小蒜也；根莖俱大而瓣多，辛而帶甘者，葫也，大蒜也。按：孫炎《爾雅正義》云：帝登山，遭蕕芋毒，將死，得蒜嚙食乃解，遂收植之，能殺腥膻蟲魚之毒。又孫《唐韻》云：張騫使西域，始得大蒜種歸。

據此則小蒜之種，自移栽，從古已有。故《爾雅》以為山蒜，所以別家蒜也。大蒜之種，自胡地移來，至漢始有。故《別錄》以葫為大蒜，所以見中國之蒜小也。

又王禎《農書》云：一種澤蒜，最易滋蔓，隨隨合。熟時采子，漫散種之。吳人調鼎多用此根作菹，更勝蔥、韭也。按：此正《別錄》所謂小蒜是也。其始自野澤移來，故有澤名，而寇氏誤作宅字矣。諸家皆以野生山蒜、澤蒜解家蒔之小蒜，皆失於詳考。小蒜雖出於，既經人力栽培，則性氣不能不移。故不得不辨。

蒜（小蒜根也）

【氣味】辛，溫，有小毒。

弘景曰：味辛性熱。損人，不可長食。

思邈曰：無毒。三月勿久食，傷人志性。黃帝書云：同生魚食，令人奪氣，陰核疼。

瑞曰：腳氣風病患，及時病後，忌食之。

【主治】歸脾腎，主霍亂，腹中不安，消穀，理胃溫中，除邪痺毒气（《別錄》）。主溪毒（弘景）。下氣，治蠱毒，敷蛇、蟲、沙虱瘡（《日華》。恭曰：此蒜與胡蔥相得。主惡毒、山溪中沙虱、水毒，大效。山人、俚獠時用之）。塗疔腫甚良（孟詵）。

葉
心煩痛，解諸毒，小兒丹疹（思邈）。

【發明】頌曰：古方多用小蒜治中冷霍亂，煮汁飲之。南齊褚澄治李道念雞瘕，便瘥。

宗奭曰：華佗用蒜齏，即此蒜也。

時珍曰：按：李延壽《南史》云：李道念病已五年。吳郡太守褚澄診之。曰：非冷非熱，當是食白瀹雞子過多也。取蒜一升煮食，吐出一物涎裹，視之乃雞雛，翅足俱全。澄曰：未盡也。更吐之，凡十二枚而愈。或以「蒜」字作「蘇」字者，誤矣。

范曄《後漢書》云：華佗見一人病噎，食不得下，令取餅店家蒜齏大酢二升飲之，立吐一蛇。病者懸蛇於車，造佗家，見壁北懸蛇數十，乃知其奇。又夏子益《奇疾方》云：人頭面上有光，他人手近之如火熾者，此中蠱也。用蒜汁半兩，和酒服之，當吐出如蛇狀。觀三書所載，則蒜乃吐蠱要藥，而後人鮮有知者。",

results.original_text:
【釋名】䔧（音歷）、澤蒜。

【集解】頌曰：江南一種山蒜，似大蒜而臭。

藏器曰：澤蒜根如小蒜，葉如韭。又生石間者名石蒜，與蒜無異。

時珍曰：山蒜、澤蒜、石蒜，同一物也，但分生於山、澤、石間不同耳。人間栽蒔小蒜，始自三種移成，故猶有澤蒜之稱。《爾雅》云：䔧，山蒜也。今京口有蒜山，產蒜是也。處處有之，不獨江南。

又呂忱《字林》云：䔧，水中蒜也。則蒜不但產於山，而又產於水也。別有山慈菇、水仙花、老鴉蒜、石蒜之類，根葉皆似蒜而不可食，其花亦異。並見草部下。

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】山蒜：治積塊，及婦人血瘕，用苦醋磨服多效（蘇頌）。澤蒜、石蒜：並溫補下氣，滑水源（藏器）。
results.translation:
**Names:** 䔧 (pronounced lì), Marsh Garlic [Zé Suàn].

**Collected Explanations:**
Sòng [Su Song] states: In the Jiangnan region there is a variety of mountain garlic [Shān Suàn] that resembles large garlic [dà suàn] but has a stronger, more pungent odor.

Zàngqì [Chen Cangqi] states: Marsh garlic [Zé Suàn] has roots like small garlic [xiǎo suàn] and leaves like chives [jiǔ]. The variety that grows among rocks is called rock garlic [Shí Suàn], and it does not differ from regular garlic.

Shízhēn [Li Shizhen] states: Mountain garlic, marsh garlic, and rock garlic are all the same plant; they differ only in that they grow in mountains, marshes, or rocky ground respectively. The cultivated small garlic [xiǎo suàn] that people grow originated from transplanting these three wild forms, which is why it still carries the name 'marsh garlic.' The Erya states: '䔧 is mountain garlic.' Indeed, there is a Garlic Mountain [Suàn Shān] at Jingkou that produces garlic of this kind. It is found everywhere, not only in Jiangnan.

Furthermore, Lǚ Chén's Zìlín states: '䔧 is garlic from the water,' indicating that garlic grows not only in the mountains but also in water. There are also other plants — mountain cimicifuga [Shān Cígū], narcissus [Shuǐxiān Huā], crow garlic [Lǎoyā Suàn], and lycoris [Shí Suàn] — whose roots and leaves all resemble garlic but are inedible, and whose flowers are also different. These are all covered in the Herbs [Cǎo] section below.

**Flavor and Nature:** Pungent [xīn], warm [wēn], non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:**
- *Mountain garlic [Shān Suàn]:* Treats accumulation masses [jī kuài] and women's blood conglomeration [xuè jiǎ]. Grinding and taking it with bitter vinegar [kǔ cù] is frequently effective. (Su Song)
- *Marsh garlic [Zé Suàn] and Rock garlic [Shí Suàn]:* Both warm and supplement [wēn bǔ], direct qi downward, and lubricate the water pathways [shuǐ yuán]. (Chen Cangqi)

entities_mentioned.name: 山蒜 (Shān Suàn, Mountain Garlic)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium macrostemon or Allium grayi — wild garlic species native to East Asia
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen treats mountain, marsh, and rock garlic as the same species growing in different habitats. Modern taxonomy may distinguish them.

entities_mentioned.name: 澤蒜 (Zé Suàn, Marsh Garlic)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium macrostemon (marsh form) or possibly Allium nipponicum
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having roots like small garlic and leaves like chives; grows in marshy ground.

entities_mentioned.name: 石蒜 (Shí Suàn, Rock Garlic) — in this entry
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium macrostemon (rock-dwelling form)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen distinguishes this rock-dwelling form as the same species as mountain and marsh garlic, not to be confused with the separate toxic plant also called Shí Suàn (Lycoris radiata) mentioned later in the text.

entities_mentioned.name: 小蒜 (Xiǎo Suàn, Small/Cultivated Garlic)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium scorodoprasum or Allium macrostemon (cultivated)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the cultivated form derived from the three wild varieties.

entities_mentioned.name: 山慈菇 (Shān Cígū)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pleione bulbocodioides or Iphigenia indica — garlic-like but inedible bulb plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a look-alike plant; inedible; treated separately in the Herbs section.

entities_mentioned.name: 水仙花 (Shuǐxiān Huā, Narcissus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Narcissus tazetta
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as a garlic look-alike with different flowers; inedible.

entities_mentioned.name: 老鴉蒜 (Lǎoyā Suàn, Crow Garlic)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycoris radiata
entities_mentioned.notes: A common folk name for Lycoris radiata in Chinese; toxic and inedible.

entities_mentioned.name: 石蒜 (Shí Suàn) — toxic variety in look-alike list
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycoris radiata (spider lily)
entities_mentioned.notes: This second mention of Shí Suàn refers to the toxic ornamental bulb Lycoris radiata, distinct from the edible rock-dwelling garlic form described earlier in the entry.

results.translator_notes: 1. The character '䔧' (OCR may render as a placeholder or blank in some versions) is glossed as pronounced 'lì' and glossed in the Erya as 'mountain garlic'; restored here based on context. 2. The medical uses extracted: (a) Mountain garlic ground with bitter vinegar [苦醋磨服] treats accumulation masses [積塊] and women's blood conglomeration [血瘕] — a gynecological condition involving palpable abdominal masses attributed to blood stasis; (b) Marsh and rock garlic warm, supplement, direct qi downward, and lubricate the water pathways — i.e., promote urination/fluid metabolism. 3. Li Shizhen's lumping of Shān Suàn, Zé Suàn, and Shí Suàn (the edible variety) as one species reflects his ecological rather than strictly taxonomic classification. 4. The 'Shí Suàn' listed among inedible look-alikes at the end of the Collected Explanations is a different plant (Lycoris) from the edible rock garlic discussed earlier — the text itself implicitly acknowledges this by directing readers to the Herbs section.

results.original_text:
【釋名】大蒜（弘景）、葷菜。

弘景曰：今人謂葫為大蒜，蒜為小蒜，以其氣類相似也。

時珍曰：按：孫 《唐韻》云：張騫使西域，始得大蒜、胡荽。則小蒜乃中土舊有，而大蒜出胡地，故有胡名。二蒜皆屬五葷，故通可稱葷。詳見蒜下。

【集解】《別錄》曰：葫，大蒜也。五月五日采，獨子者入藥尤佳。

弘景曰：今人謂葫為大蒜，蒜為小蒜，以其氣類相似也。

時珍曰：按：孫《唐韻》云：張騫使西域，始得大蒜、胡荽。則小蒜乃中土舊有，而大蒜出胡地，故有胡名。二蒜皆屬五葷，故通可稱葷。詳見蒜下。

時珍曰：大、小二蒜皆八月種。春食苗，夏初食苔，五月食根，秋月收種。北人不可一日無者也。

【氣味】辛，溫，有毒。久食損人目。弘景曰：性最熏臭，不可食。俗人作齏以啖肉，損性伐命，莫此之甚。惟可生食，不中煮也。恭曰：此物煮羹為饌中之俊，而陶云不中煮，當是未經試耳。

藏器曰：初食不利目，多食卻明。久食令人血清，使毛發白。

時珍曰：久食傷肝損眼。故嵇康《養生論》云：葷辛害目，此為甚耳。今北人嗜蒜宿炕，故盲瞽最多。陳氏乃云多食明目，與《別錄》相左，何耶？

震亨曰：大蒜屬火，性熱喜散，快膈，善化肉，暑月人多食之。傷氣之禍，積久自見，養生者忌之。化肉之功，不足論也。

瑞曰：多食傷肺、傷脾、傷肝膽，生痰助火昏神。

思邈曰：四月、八月食葫，傷神，令人喘悸，脅肋氣急，口味多爽。多食生葫行房，傷肝氣，令人面無色。生葫合青魚食，令人腹內生瘡，腸中腫，又成疝瘕，發黃疾。合蜜食，殺人。凡服一切補藥，不可食之。

【主治】歸五臟，散癰腫瘡，除風邪，殺毒氣（《別錄》）。下氣，消穀，化肉（蘇恭）。去水惡瘴氣，除風濕，破冷氣，爛癖，伏邪惡，宣通溫補，療瘡癬，殺鬼去痛（藏器）。健脾胃，治腎氣，止霍亂轉筋腹痛，除邪祟，解溫疫，去蠱毒，療勞瘧冷風，敷風損冷痛，惡瘡、蛇蟲、溪毒、沙虱，並搗貼之。熟醋浸，經年者良（《日華》）。溫水搗爛服，治中暑不醒。搗貼足心，止鼻衄不止。和豆豉丸服，治暴下血，通水道（宗奭）。搗汁飲，治吐血心痛。煮汁飲，治角弓反張。同鯽魚丸，治膈氣。同蛤粉丸，治水腫。同黃丹丸，治痢瘧、孕痢。同乳香丸，治腹痛。搗膏敷臍，能達下焦，消水，利大小便。貼足心，能引熱下行，治泄瀉暴痢及乾濕霍亂，止衄血。納肛中，能通幽門，治關格不通（時珍）。

【發明】宗奭曰：葫氣極葷，置臭肉中反能掩臭。凡中暑毒人，爛嚼三、兩瓣，溫水送之，下咽即知，但禁飲冷水。又鼻衄不止者，搗貼足心，衄止即拭去。

【集解】時珍曰：五辛菜，乃元旦立春，以蔥、蒜、韭、蓼、蒿、芥辛嫩之菜，雜和食之，取迎
新之義，謂之五辛盤，杜甫詩所謂：“春日春盤細生菜”是矣。

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

藏器曰：熱病後食，多損目。

【主治】歲朝食之，助發五臟氣。常食，溫中去惡氣，消食下氣（藏器）。
